> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formal Definition (Syllabus Standard)**
> Sprinkler irrigation is a pressurized irrigation method in which water is sprayed into the air through a network of pipes and **nozzles (sprinkler heads)** in the form of **artificial rainfall**, simulating natural rain. Water is distributed through plastic (PVC / HDPE / LLDPE) pipes under a pressure of **1.5 to 4.0 kg/cm²** (≈ 15 to 40 m head) and discharged through rotating or fixed nozzles covering a wetted diameter of **6 to 30 metres** depending on nozzle size and pressure.

### Conceptual Analogy — Artificial Rain over the Field

Imagine standing in your garden on a hot afternoon. Suddenly someone installs a rotating spray head above your plants, and water shoots up in a beautiful arc, falling uniformly over the rose bushes, lawn grass, and flower beds — exactly like **natural rain**, but you can turn it on or off whenever you want. That is a sprinkler system. Instead of waiting for the monsoon, you manufacture your own rainfall on demand, distributing water through the air to cover a circular wetted area around each sprinkler head.

## 1.3 Why Plastics? — The Role of Plastic Materials in Irrigation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Plastics Make Modern Irrigation Possible**
> Almost every component of modern micro-irrigation systems — laterals, mainlines, submains, emitters, microtubes, filters, and sprinkler bodies — is made of **engineering plastics** such as **Low Density Polyethylene (LDPE)**, **Linear Low Density Polyethylene (LLDPE)**, **High Density Polyethylene (HDPE)**, **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)**, and **Polypropylene (PP)**. These plastics are corrosion-proof, lightweight, UV-stabilized, chemically resistant to fertilizers, and can be **extruded, injection-moulded, or blow-moulded** into precise shapes. Without plastics, the uniform emitters, thin-walled laterals, and durable sprinkler bodies that make pressurized irrigation economical would simply not exist.

## 1.4 Comparative Snapshot

| Property | Drip Irrigation | Sprinkler Irrigation |
|---|---|---|
| Working pressure | **0.5 – 1.5 kg/cm²** | **1.5 – 4.0 kg/cm²** |
| Application efficiency | **80 – 95 %** | **60 – 75 %** |
| Water state at outlet | Liquid drops | Artificial rain (spray) |
| Suitable terrain | Undulating, slopy | Level to moderately slopy |
| Wind effect | Negligible | High (drift losses) |
| Typical discharge | **1 – 16 lph per emitter** | **0.5 – 5 lps per sprinkler** |
| Common plastic | LDPE / LLDPE laterals | HDPE / PVC risers & mains |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Drip Irrigation System — Components & Working Principle

A drip system operates by transporting water from a **pump / overhead tank** through a series of progressively smaller plastic pipes, finally releasing it through emitters at the plant root zone.

### Working Steps (Logic Flow)
1. **Water Source** → river, well, pond, or storage tank.
2. **Pump / Head Unit** → provides the required pressure (usually a centrifugal pump).
3. **Fertigation Tank + Venturi / Injector** → allows mixing of water-soluble fertilizer into the irrigation stream.
4. **Filter Station** → **screen filter / disc filter / sand filter / hydrocyclone** removes suspended particles that would otherwise block emitters. *Emitter clogging is the single biggest cause of drip-system failure.*
5. **Control Valves** → **gate valves, ball valves, pressure-regulating valves, air-release valves, and non-return valves** maintain the system pressure and prevent backflow.
6. **Mainline (PVC / HDPE)** → carries water from the head unit to the sub-main.
7. **Sub-main (PVC / HDPE, smaller diameter)** → distributes water to the manifold / valve assembly of each subunit.
8. **Manifold (PVC)** → connects to the laterals.
9. **Laterals (LDPE / LLDPE, 12 mm or 16 mm OD)** → run along each row of plants.
10. **Emitters (Drippers)** → discharge water at a controlled rate directly to the root zone.

### Types of Emitters (High-Yield for KTU)
- **Online (point-source) drippers** — inserted into the lateral wall at desired spacing.
- **Inline drippers** — factory-inserted inside the lateral at fixed spacing (e.g., 30 cm, 40 cm, 50 cm).
- **Pressure-compensating (PC) drippers** — maintain nearly constant discharge across a pressure range, using a flexible silicone diaphragm.
- **Non-pressure-compensating (NPC) drippers** — discharge varies with pressure; cheaper.
- **Micro-sprinklers / micro-jets** — small sprinklers used on tree crops.

### Plastic Material Mapping (Exam Favorite)
| Component | Preferred Plastic | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Mainline / Sub-main | **PVC / HDPE** | High pressure rating, rigid, durable |
| Lateral | **LDPE / LLDPE** | Flexible, can be coiled, UV-stabilized |
| Emitter body | **PP / POM / HDPE** | Injection-mouldable, chemically resistant |
| Diaphragm (PC emitter) | **Silicone rubber** | Elastic, fatigue-resistant |
| Filter housing | **PP / FRP** | Corrosion-proof |
| Sprinkler body | **Engineering plastic / brass nozzle** | UV-stable, impact-resistant |

## 2.2 Sprinkler Irrigation System — Components & Working Principle

### Working Steps
1. **Pumping Station** → must generate **2.5 – 4.0 kg/cm²** pressure for impact sprinklers.
2. **Mainline (HDPE / PVC, Class-3 or Class-4)** → high-pressure underground pipe.
3. **Sub-mainline** → distributes to hydrants.
4. **Hydrant / Riser Pipe (PVC / GI)** → vertical pipe rising out of the ground.
5. **Sprinkler Head Assembly** → contains the **body, bearing, nozzle, and impact arm**.
6. **Nozzle** → converts pressure energy into kinetic energy, producing a high-velocity jet.
7. **Impact Arm** → deflects the jet, causing the head to rotate, distributing water in a circle.

### Types of Sprinklers
- **Impact (percussion / hammer) sprinklers** — most common, single or double nozzle.
- **Rotary sprinklers** — gear-driven.
- **Spray heads (fixed / pop-up)** — used in lawns and landscaping.
- **Micro-sprinklers** — discharge **30–200 lph**, used in orchards.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear every year in KTU ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Discharge through an emitter / nozzle (orifice equation) | $q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$ | $q$ = discharge (m³/s), $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge, $A$ = orifice area (m²), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $h$ = pressure head (m) | Emitter hydraulics, sprinkler nozzle |
| 2 | Discharge in volumetric units | $q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2 g H}$ | $q$ in lph if $a$ in mm² and $H$ in m (with conversion factor) | Direct lph calculation |
| 3 | Darcy–Weisbach head loss (friction in pipes) | $h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^{2}}{2 g}$ | $f$ = friction factor, $L$ = pipe length (m), $D$ = diameter (m), $V$ = velocity (m/s) | Both drip & sprinkler mains |
| 4 | Hazen–Williams head loss | $h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$ | $Q$ = discharge (m³/s), $C$ = HW coefficient (140 for PVC, 130 for HDPE) | Plastic pipe design |
| 5 | Velocity of flow | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi D^{2}}$ | $Q$ in m³/s, $D$ in m | Pipe sizing |
| 6 | Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (UC) | $UC = 100 \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{q}}\right]$ | $q_i$ = individual emitter discharge, $\bar{q}$ = mean discharge, $n$ = no. of emitters | Drip system performance |
| 7 | Distribution Uniformity (DU) | $DU = 100 \cdot \dfrac{\bar{q}_{25}}{\bar{q}}$ | $\bar{q}_{25}$ = mean of lowest 25 % of discharges | Field evaluation |
| 8 | Precipitation rate of sprinkler | $P = \dfrac{q \cdot 3600}{S_l \cdot S_m}$ | $P$ in mm/h, $q$ in lps, $S_l$ and $S_m$ in metres | Sprinkler layout design |
| 9 | Wetted radius of impact sprinkler | $R = 1.35 \cdot \sqrt{d \cdot H}$ | $d$ = nozzle diameter (mm), $H$ = pressure head (m), $R$ in m | Approximate design |
| 10 | Application rate (depth) | $d = \dfrac{Q \cdot t}{A}$ | $d$ in mm, $Q$ in lps, $t$ in seconds, $A$ in m² | Both systems |
| 11 | Travel speed of lateral-move system | $S = \dfrac{L \cdot Q_{lat}}{q_{appl} \cdot W \cdot L}$ | $L$ in m, $W$ in m | Mechanized sprinklers |
| 12 | Number of emitters per plant | $n = \dfrac{\text{Required depth}}{\text{Emitter discharge} \times \text{Time}}$ | dimensionless | Drip design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Reminder**
> $1 \text{ lph} = \dfrac{1}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$ and $1 \text{ lps} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$. Pressure of **1 kg/cm² ≈ 10 m of water head ≈ 98.066 kPa**.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Water conservation agriculture** — 40–60 % water savings over flood irrigation.
- **Greenhouse / polyhouse cultivation** — drip integrates with fertigation to deliver precise nutrient solutions.
- **Landscape engineering** — pop-up sprinklers for lawns, golf courses.
- **Tree-crop irrigation** — micro-sprinklers for coconut, mango, areca nut plantations in Kerala.
- **Soil-erosion control** — sprinkler covers slopes where drip lines are difficult.
- **Plastic piping industry** — drives the demand for **PVC, HDPE, LDPE, LLDPE** pipes and injection-moulded emitters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Discharge Through an Emitter (Orifice Equation)

### Statement
To show that the discharge through a drip emitter or sprinkler nozzle follows the classical **torricellian orifice equation** with a discharge coefficient $C_d$.

### Assumptions
1. Water is an **incompressible, ideal fluid** (we add $C_d$ to correct for viscosity).
2. The emitter/nozzle acts as a **small orifice** with sharp or rounded edges.
3. Pressure head $H$ above the orifice remains constant during discharge.
4. Velocity of approach at the entrance of the orifice is negligible.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation** between the upstream water surface (point 1) and the vena-contracta of the orifice (point 2):

$$
\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the boundary conditions:**
- $P_1 = P_2 = P_{atm}$ (both points exposed to atmosphere).
- $V_1 \approx 0$ (large upstream tank).
- $z_1 - z_2 = H$ (head driving the flow).

Substituting:

$$
H \;=\; \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2g}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the theoretical velocity at the vena-contracta (Torricelli's theorem):**

$$
V_2 \;=\; \sqrt{2 g H}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the actual cross-sectional area of the jet $A$ to obtain the theoretical discharge:**

$$
q_{th} \;=\; A \cdot \sqrt{2 g H}
$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the coefficient of discharge $C_d$** to account for viscous losses and vena-contracta contraction (since the jet area at vena-contracta is less than the orifice area):

$$
q_{act} \;=\; C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 g H}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,q \;=\; C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 g H}\,}
$$

### Engineering Note
For drip emitters, typical $C_d$ ranges from **0.6 to 0.7**. For sprinkler nozzles, $C_d$ is around **0.85 to 0.95** because nozzles are convergent and well-shaped.

## 3.2 Derivation — Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient

### Statement
To derive an expression that quantifies how uniformly all emitters in a drip system discharge water.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the mean discharge of $n$ emitters:**

$$
\bar{q} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i
$$

**Step 2 — Define the absolute deviation of each emitter from the mean:**

$$
\Delta q_i \;=\; \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert
$$

**Step 3 — Define the average deviation:**

$$
\overline{\Delta q} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta q_i \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert
$$

**Step 4 — Define uniformity as the fraction of mean that is *not* lost to deviation:**

$$
\dfrac{\bar{q} - \overline{\Delta q}}{\bar{q}} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{\overline{\Delta q}}{\bar{q}}
$$

**Step 5 — Express as a percentage to obtain Christiansen's UC:**

$$
\boxed{\,UC \;=\; 100 \cdot \left[ 1 - \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{q}} \right]\,}
$$

### Engineering Interpretation
A well-designed drip system should have **UC ≥ 85 %**. Values below 80 % indicate poor hydraulic design, clogged emitters, or excessive pressure variation along the lateral.

## 3.3 Derivation — Precipitation Rate of a Sprinkler

### Step 1 — Discharge of one sprinkler** in litres per second is $q$ (lps).

**Step 2 — Area covered** by one sprinkler at spacing $S_l \times S_m$ (m²) is:

$$
A_{cov} \;=\; S_l \cdot S_m
$$

**Step 3 — Total volume of water applied per hour:**

$$
V \;=\; q \cdot 3600 \quad \text{(litres per hour)}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert volume to depth** (1 litre per m² = 1 mm depth):

$$
P \;=\; \dfrac{V}{A_{cov}} \;=\; \dfrac{q \cdot 3600}{S_l \cdot S_m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P \;=\; \dfrac{3600 \, q}{S_l \, S_m} \quad \text{mm/h}\,}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples (For Board Practice)

### Example 1 — Drip Emitter Discharge
A drip emitter has an orifice area $A = 4 \times 10^{-7}$ m² (≈ 0.71 mm diameter) and operates at a head of 10 m. Given $C_d = 0.65$, compute the discharge in **lph**.

**Solution:**

$$
q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 g H}
$$

$$
q = 0.65 \times 4 \times 10^{-7} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 10}
$$

$$
q = 0.65 \times 4 \times 10^{-7} \times \sqrt{196.2}
$$

$$
q = 0.65 \times 4 \times 10^{-7} \times 14.005
$$

$$
q = 3.6413 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

Convert to lph ($1 \, m^3/s = 3.6 \times 10^{6}$ lph):

$$
q = 3.6413 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} \approx 13.11 \; \text{lph}
$$

**Answer:** $q \approx 13.1$ lph — this is a standard 16 lph-class emitter operating at 10 m head.

### Example 2 — Precipitation Rate of Sprinkler
A sprinkler discharges $0.6$ lps. Layout spacing is $12 \text{ m} \times 12 \text{ m}$. Compute precipitation rate.

$$
P = \dfrac{3600 \times 0.6}{12 \times 12} = \dfrac{2160}{144} = 15 \; \text{mm/h}
$$

**Answer:** $P = 15$ mm/h. This must be **less than the soil infiltration rate** to avoid runoff.

### Example 3 — Uniformity Coefficient
Emitters in a drip line measured discharges (in lph): 4.0, 3.8, 4.2, 3.6, 3.9, 4.1, 3.7, 4.0. Compute UC.

**Solution:**

Mean: $\bar{q} = (4.0+3.8+4.2+3.6+3.9+4.1+3.7+4.0)/8 = 31.3/8 = 3.9125$ lph.

Sum of absolute deviations: $|4.0-3.9125|+|3.8-3.9125|+|4.2-3.9125|+|3.6-3.9125|+|3.9-3.9125|+|4.1-3.9125|+|3.7-3.9125|+|4.0-3.9125|$
$= 0.0875+0.1125+0.2875+0.3125+0.0125+0.1875+0.2125+0.0875 = 1.3000$

$$
UC = 100 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{1.3}{8 \times 3.9125}\right) = 100 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{1.3}{31.3}\right) = 100 \times (1 - 0.0415) \approx 95.85\%
$$

**Answer:** UC ≈ **95.85 %** — excellent uniformity (above the 85 % benchmark).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Drip Irrigation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SOURCE\nWELL OR POND] --> B[PUMPING UNIT\nCENTRIFUGAL PUMP]
    B --> C[FERTIGATION TANK\nVENTURI INJECTOR]
    C --> D[FILTER STATION\nSCREEN OR DISC OR SAND]
    D --> E[AIR RELEASE VALVE\nAND PRESSURE GAUGE]
    E --> F[CONTROL VALVE\nBALL OR GATE]
    F --> G[MAINLINE\nPVC OR HDPE]
    G --> H[SUBMAIN\nPVC OR HDPE]
    H --> I[MANIFOLD\nPVC]
    I --> J[LATERAL 1\nLDPE 16MM]
    I --> K[LATERAL 2\nLDPE 16MM]
    I --> L[LATERAL 3\nLDPE 16MM]
    J --> M[EMITTER 1\nDRIPPER 4 LPH]
    J --> N[EMITTER 2\nDRIPPER 4 LPH]
    K --> O[EMITTER 3\nDRIPPER 4 LPH]
    L --> P[EMITTER 4\nDRIPPER 4 LPH]
    M --> Q[ROOT ZONE\nPLANT 1]
    N --> R[ROOT ZONE\nPLANT 2]
    O --> S[ROOT ZONE\nPLANT 3]
    P --> T[ROOT ZONE\nPLANT 4]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Sprinkler Irrigation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PUMPING UNIT\nHIGH HEAD 2.5 TO 4 KG PER CM2] --> B[BY PASS VALVE\nAND PRESSURE GAUGE]
    B --> C[MAINLINE\nHDPE OR PVC CLASS 4]
    C --> D[SUBMAIN\nHDPE OR PVC CLASS 3]
    D --> E[HYDRANT\nRISER PIPE]
    D --> F[HYDRANT\nRISER PIPE]
    D --> G[HYDRANT\nRISER PIPE]
    E --> H[IMPACT SPRINKLER 1\nNOZZLE 4 MM]
    F --> I[IMPACT SPRINKLER 2\nNOZZLE 4 MM]
    G --> J[IMPACT SPRINKLER 3\nNOZZLE 4 MM]
    H --> K[WETTED CIRCLE 1\nRADIUS 12 M]
    I --> L[WETTED CIRCLE 2\nRADIUS 12 M]
    J --> M[WETTED CIRCLE 3\nRADIUS 12 M]
    K --> N[OVERLAP ZONE\nUNIFORM APPLICATION]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[CROP ROOT ZONE\nARTIFICIAL RAINFALL]
```

## 4.3 Component Cross-Reference Map (Drip System)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[PRESSURIZED IRRIGATION SYSTEM] --> B[HEAD UNIT]
    A --> C[DISTRIBUTION NETWORK]
    A --> D[FIELD DELIVERY UNIT]
    B --> B1[PUMP]
    B --> B2[FERTIGATION UNIT]
    B --> B3[FILTERS]
    B --> B4[VALVES AND GAUGES]
    C --> C1[MAINLINE PVC OR HDPE]
    C --> C2[SUBMAIN PVC OR HDPE]
    C --> C3[MANIFOLD PVC]
    C --> C4[LATERAL LDPE OR LLDPE]
    D --> D1[ONLINE DRIPPER]
    D --> D2[INLINE DRIPPER]
    D --> D3[PC DRIPPER]
    D --> D4[MICRO SPRINKLER]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Hydraulic Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ELECTRICAL OR DIESEL ENERGY] --> B[PUMP]
    B --> C[PRESSURE ENERGY\n0.5 TO 4 KG PER CM2]
    C --> D[FRICTION LOSS IN PIPES\nDARCY WEISBACH OR HAZEN WILLIAMS]
    C --> E[MINOR LOSSES\nELBOWS VALVES TEES]
    D --> F[NET HEAD AVAILABLE AT EMITTER]
    E --> F
    F --> G[VELOCITY AT EMITTER OR NOZZLE]
    G --> H[ORIFICE DISCHARGE\nQ = CD A SQRT 2GH]
    H --> I[SOIL MOISTURE AT ROOT ZONE]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**List any six advantages of drip irrigation over conventional surface irrigation.**

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. **Water saving** — 40–60 % water savings over flood irrigation.
2. **Higher application efficiency** — 80–95 % compared to 30–40 % for flood.
3. **Improved crop yield** — uniform water supply reduces plant stress.
4. **Weed control** — only root zone is wetted, inter-row remains dry.
5. **Fertigation possible** — fertilizer applied directly through the system, saving 30 % nutrients.
6. **Suitable for undulating terrain and poor soils** — without land levelling.

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between drip and sprinkler irrigation systems (any six points).**

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

| Sl. | Drip Irrigation | Sprinkler Irrigation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Water applied at root zone as drops | Water sprayed in air as artificial rain |
| 2 | Pressure 0.5 – 1.5 kg/cm² | Pressure 1.5 – 4.0 kg/cm² |
| 3 | Efficiency 80 – 95 % | Efficiency 60 – 75 % |
| 4 | Discharge 1 – 16 lph per emitter | Discharge 0.5 – 5 lps per sprinkler |
| 5 | No wind effect | Affected by wind drift |
| 6 | Suitable for orchards & row crops | Suitable for field crops & lawns |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the various components of a drip irrigation system with a neat functional diagram. List the engineering plastics used for each component.

**Model Solution:**

A drip irrigation system consists of the **head unit, distribution network, and field delivery unit**.

**1. Head Unit (2 marks):**
- **Pumping unit** — centrifugal pump supplies 0.5 – 1.5 kg/cm² head. Usually electric motor driven; in Kerala, both monoblock and submersible pumps are used.
- **Fertigation equipment** — venturi injector, fertilizer tank, or dosing pump. Allows water-soluble fertilizers (urea, KNO₃, 19-19-19 NPK) to be injected.
- **Filtration system** — *screen filter* (100 – 200 mesh) for fine sand, *disc filter* for organic debris, *sand filter* for silt-laden pond water, *hydrocyclone* for sand separation. *Filters are the heart of a drip system.*
- **Control valves** — gate valve, ball valve, air-release valve, non-return valve, pressure-regulating valve, flow-control valve.

**2. Distribution Network (2 marks):**
- **Mainline** — Class-4 PVC or HDPE pipe conveying water from head to sub-main; usually buried 60 cm below ground; takes the highest pressure.
- **Sub-main** — Class-3 PVC pipe branching from mainline; carries water to manifolds.
- **Manifold** — small PVC pipe connecting sub-main to laterals; usually equipped with a control valve for each subunit.
- **Laterals** — **LDPE / LLDPE pipes of 12 mm or 16 mm OD**, flexible, UV-stabilized, black-coloured (to prevent algal growth), laid along each crop row.

**3. Field Delivery Unit (1 mark):**
- **Drippers / emitters** — discharge 1–16 lph. Types: online, inline, pressure-compensating (PC), non-PC, and button drippers.

**Plastic Material Mapping (1 mark):**

| Component | Plastic Used |
|---|---|
| Mainline | PVC / HDPE |
| Sub-main | PVC |
| Lateral | LDPE / LLDPE |
| Emitter body | PP / HDPE / POM |
| Filter housing | PP / FRP |
| Diaphragm | Silicone rubber |

**Functional Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in SECTION 4.1 above.

#### (b) [7 Marks] A drip emitter has a 1.0 mm diameter circular orifice. Compute the discharge in lph if the operating head is 8 m. Take $C_d$ = 0.65. Comment on whether this is a standard 4 lph-class emitter.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert diameter to area:**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3})^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the orifice equation:**

$$
q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 g H}
$$

$$
q = 0.65 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-7} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 8}
$$

$$
q = 0.65 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-7} \times \sqrt{156.96}
$$

$$
q = 0.65 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-7} \times 12.528
$$

$$
q = 6.397 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to lph** ($1 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} = 3.6 \times 10^{6}$ lph):

$$
q = 6.397 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} \approx 23.03 \text{ lph}
$$

**Conclusion:** The emitter discharges ≈ **23 lph**, which is much higher than the standard **4 lph** class. This indicates a larger orifice or higher head. The standard 4 lph-class emitter would typically have a smaller orifice (≈ 0.6 mm) or a labyrinth channel that dissipates pressure through turbulent flow.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] — *Alternative Choice*

#### (a) [7 Marks] Describe the design layout of a sprinkler irrigation system. Discuss the merits and demerits of sprinkler irrigation in detail.

**Model Solution:**

**1. System Layout (3 marks):**
The components are arranged as follows:
- **Source & Pump** — delivers 2.5 – 4.0 kg/cm² pressure.
- **Mainline** — buried HDPE / PVC Class-4 pipe (63 mm – 110 mm OD).
- **Sub-main** — HDPE / PVC Class-3 pipe branching to hydrants.
- **Hydrants** — quick-coupling riser pipes, usually 25 mm – 40 mm diameter.
- **Sprinkler heads** — impact-type with single/double nozzle, mounted 1.5 – 2.5 m above ground on a riser pipe.
- **Layout pattern** — square or triangular; triangular layout gives more uniform coverage.

**2. Merits (2 marks):**
- Suitable for crops that cannot tolerate standing water (wheat, groundnut).
- Covers large areas quickly.
- Can be used on **sloping or undulating land** without land shaping.
- Provides **frost protection** by raising air humidity.
- Less labour-intensive than flood irrigation.
- Reduces soil erosion compared to flood.

**3. Demerits (2 marks):**
- **High initial cost** — pump, pipes, sprinklers.
- **High operating cost** — energy to generate 2–4 kg/cm² pressure.
- **Wind drift losses** — up to 15–25 % water loss in strong winds.
- **High evaporation losses** — especially in hot dry climates; not suitable for Kerala midday.
- **Sensitive to clogging** if water has suspended solids.
- **Leaf burn** risk when saline water evaporates on foliage.

#### (b) [7 Marks] A sprinkler discharges 0.8 lps at 30 m head. The sprinklers are laid out in a triangular pattern with spacing 12 m × 14 m. Compute (i) precipitation rate, and (ii) wetted radius using $R = 1.35\sqrt{d \cdot H}$ with $d$ = nozzle diameter = 5 mm.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Precipitation rate:**

For a triangular layout, the equivalent area per sprinkler is:

$$
A_{cov} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot S_l \cdot S_m = \dfrac{1.732}{2} \times 12 \times 14 = 145.49 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{3600 \times q}{A_{cov}} = \dfrac{3600 \times 0.8}{145.49} = \dfrac{2880}{145.49} \approx 19.8 \text{ mm/h}
$$

**Note:** If a simple square-layout formula is assumed (as many textbooks do), $P = 3600 q / (S_l S_m) = 2880/168 = 17.14$ mm/h. Both answers should be accepted by the examiner.

**(ii) Wetted radius:**

$$
R = 1.35 \times \sqrt{d \cdot H} = 1.35 \times \sqrt{5 \times 30} = 1.35 \times \sqrt{150}
$$

$$
R = 1.35 \times 12.247 \approx 16.53 \text{ m}
$$

**Conclusion:** Each sprinkler covers a wetted diameter of ≈ 33 m, comfortably covering the 12 m × 14 m spacing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — examiners very often deduct 1 mark when a student writes discharge in lph but forgets to multiply by $3.6 \times 10^{6}$ from m³/s. Always show the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing sprinkler and drip formulas** — many students wrongly apply $R = 1.35\sqrt{dH}$ to drip emitters. That formula is **only for impact sprinklers**.
> 3. **Forgetting the pressure head term** — many students write $q = C_d A \sqrt{2gH}$ but then plug in **pressure in kg/cm²** without first converting to metres of water head. Always convert: $1 \text{ kg/cm}^2 = 10 \text{ m of water head}$.
> 4. **UC formula mistakes** — students forget the absolute value bars or divide by $\bar{q}$ instead of $n\bar{q}$. Write $n\bar{q}$ explicitly.
> 5. **Triangular layout area** — students use $S_l \times S_m$ instead of $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} S_l S_m$ for triangular sprinkler layouts.
> 6. **Not naming plastics** — a question asking "what plastic is used" that gets answered "plastic" without specifying *LDPE/LLDPE/HDPE/PVC/PP* typically gets 0 marks in KTU valuation.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Drip irrigation** delivers water at **1–16 lph** at the root zone through emitters, working at **0.5–1.5 kg/cm²**.
- **Sprinkler irrigation** sprays water as artificial rain through nozzles, working at **1.5–4.0 kg/cm²**.
- The **orifice discharge equation** $q = C_d A \sqrt{2gH}$ is the *mother equation* of both systems.
- **Christiansen's UC** quantifies emitter uniformity — **UC ≥ 85 %** is the design benchmark.
- **Precipitation rate** of a sprinkler must be **less than the soil's basic infiltration rate** to avoid runoff.
- **Hazen-Williams** equation is preferred for plastic pipes; use **C = 140 for PVC, 130 for HDPE**.
- **Plastic materials** — LDPE/LLDPE for laterals (flexible), PVC/HDPE for mains (rigid), PP/POM for emitter bodies, silicone for PC diaphragms.
- **Filters** are the *heart* of a drip system; their failure causes **emitter clogging**, the most common drip-system failure.
- **Wind** is the biggest enemy of sprinkler systems — avoids sprinkler irrigation in strong wind periods.
- **Fertigation** (fertilizer + irrigation) is possible only in drip and sprinkler systems, not in flood.
- **Triangular layout** of sprinklers gives more uniform coverage than square layout.
- Conversion: **1 kg/cm² = 10 m of water head = 98.066 kPa**.
- Conversion: **1 lph = 1/3,600,000 m³/s**.
- Conversion: **1 litre per m² = 1 mm depth of water applied**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Polymers in Control of Percolation Losses in Fields
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT611 - PLASTIC APPLICATION IN AGRICULTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Use of Polymers in Control of Percolation Losses in Fields

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Percolation Loss

In soil hydrology and irrigation engineering, **percolation loss** is defined as the *downward movement of water through the soil profile below the effective root zone of crops, resulting in the unrecoverable loss of applied irrigation water or rainfall to deep subsoil strata and the groundwater table*.

From the KTU 2024 Scheme perspective (Course Code: OEAGT611), the **use of polymers in control of percolation losses** refers to the engineered application of synthetic polymer materials (such as polyolefin films, polyacrylamide hydrogels, and sodium polyacrylate superabsorbents) to physically obstruct, chemically bind, or hydraulically restrict the gravitational flow of water through the soil matrix, thereby increasing the *field application efficiency* and *water use efficiency (WUE)* of agricultural land.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Percolation is one of the three major *conveyance* and *application* losses in surface irrigation (the others being *runoff* and *evaporation*). KTU examiners frequently test the distinction between percolation, seepage, and deep drainage.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine pouring a full glass of water onto a *dry sandy beach*. Within seconds, the water vanishes into the sand — you cannot recover it, and it carries valuable dissolved nutrients (fertilizers) far below where plant roots can reach. This lost water is your **percolation loss**.

Now, place a **thin transparent plastic sheet (polyethylene film)** beneath the sand layer. When you pour water again, it now *pools* on top of the sheet and remains available for the plant roots. This is exactly what an *underground polymer barrier* or *plastic-lined farm pond* achieves in real agriculture.

Alternatively, think of *superabsorbent polymers (SAPs)* as **tiny "water sponges"** mixed into the soil. When it rains or you irrigate, these polymer crystals absorb hundreds of times their own weight in water, locking it into a gel that roots can later extract — but which gravity cannot pull downward into deep subsoil.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following standard metrics are used to quantify polymer-based percolation control performance:

- **Hydraulic Conductivity (K)**: typically expressed in **cm/hr** or **m/day**; values for PE film liners are in the order of **$10^{-12}$ to $10^{-14}$ cm/s**.
- **Superabsorbent Polymer Absorption Capacity**: typically **300 to 1000 g of water per g of dry polymer**.
- **Water Use Efficiency (WUE)**: ratio of crop yield (kg) to water consumed (m³).
- **Percolation Reduction Efficiency (PRE)**: percentage reduction in percolation rate after polymer intervention.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world benchmark:** Properly installed 0.5 mm HDPE pond liners reduce seepage by **95% to 99%** compared to unlined earthen ponds. SAPs at application rates of **2 to 5 kg/m²** can increase the water-holding capacity of sandy soils by **30% to 50%**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory: How Polymers Intercept Percolation

Polymers control percolation through three distinct physical-chemical mechanisms, each corresponding to a specific polymer class:

### Mechanism A — Physical Barrier (Plastic Films & Geo-membranes)
- **Polymer type:** Polyethylene (LDPE, HDPE), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Polypropylene (PP), Ethylene Propylene Diene Monomer (EPDM).
- **Action:** Forms an impermeable membrane at the soil-water interface (canal/pond bottom, sub-surface, or above soil as mulch). The polymer's near-zero hydraulic conductivity (K → 0) blocks gravitational flux.
- **Why it works:** Darcy's Law dictates that flux is proportional to K. By replacing the high-K soil with a film whose K is 10–12 orders of magnitude lower, flux is effectively eliminated.

### Mechanism B — Chemical Retention (Superabsorbent Polymers)
- **Polymer type:** Cross-linked polyacrylamide (PAM), Sodium polyacrylate (SPA), Polyacrylate-co-acrylamide copolymers.
- **Action:** Hydrophilic functional groups (–COONa, –CONH₂) along the polymer backbone form hydrogen bonds with water molecules, immobilizing them in a 3D hydrogel network.
- **Why it works:** Bound water resists gravity because it is held by *matric potential* far exceeding the gravitational potential driving percolation. The polymer effectively rewrites the soil's *soil water characteristic curve (SWCC)*.

### Mechanism C — Soil Structure Modification (Polymer Conditioners)
- **Polymer type:** Linear/cross-linked anionic polyacrylamide (PAM), Hydrolyzed polyacrylonitrile.
- **Action:** Polymer chains adsorb onto clay and silt particles, flocculating them into stable aggregates. This increases effective pore size variability, creating more *mesopores* (which hold plant-available water) and fewer *macropores* (which conduct percolation).
- **Why it works:** Bulk density decreases, total porosity increases, but the *macroporosity* (responsible for rapid drainage) drops sharply.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Legend | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$ | $Q$ = discharge, $K$ = hydraulic conductivity, $i$ = hydraulic gradient, $A$ = area | m³/s | Darcy's Law — foundation for all seepage calculations |
| 2 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = K \cdot i$ | $v$ = Darcy flux (percolation velocity) | m/s | Specific discharge / percolation rate |
| 3 | $K_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\sum K_i \cdot d_i}{D}$ | $K_i$ = layer hydraulic conductivity, $d_i$ = layer thickness, $D$ = total depth | m/s | Effective K of multi-layer (soil + liner) system |
| 4 | $P_{\text{red}} = \dfrac{P_u - P_l}{P_u} \times 100$ | $P_u$ = unlined percolation, $P_l$ = lined percolation | % | Percolation Reduction Efficiency (PRE) |
| 5 | $W_s = (P_u - P_l) \cdot A \cdot t$ | $W_s$ = water saved, $A$ = area, $t$ = time interval | m³ | Total water saved over season |
| 6 | $W_r = M_{\text{SAP}} \cdot C_{\text{abs}} \cdot \eta$ | $M_{\text{SAP}}$ = polymer mass, $C_{\text{abs}}$ = absorption capacity, $\eta$ = field efficiency factor | g of water | Water retained by SAP in root zone |
| 7 | $S_{\text{film}} = K_{\text{film}} \cdot \dfrac{h}{t_{\text{film}}}$ | $S_{\text{film}}$ = seepage through film, $h$ = hydraulic head, $t_{\text{film}}$ = film thickness | m³/m²/day | Seepage through plastic liner |
| 8 | $i = \dfrac{h}{L}$ | $h$ = head difference, $L$ = flow path length | dimensionless | Hydraulic gradient definition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** In the KTU Formula Sheet table, all absolute-value and division bars use **$\vert$** or **$\mid$** symbols to preserve markdown table integrity. **Never** insert a raw vertical pipe **$\vert$** inside a table cell.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The polymer-percolation control system is a cornerstone technology in:
- **Precision irrigation** (drip and sprinkler systems in arid zones of Rajasthan, Kutch, and West Asia).
- **Water harvesting structures** (lined farm ponds under the *Jal Sanchay* and *PMKSY* schemes of the Government of India).
- **Greenhouse and polyhouse cultivation** (where HDPE ground covers prevent sub-soil leaching of expensive soluble N-P-K fertilizers).
- **Landfill and contaminated-soil remediation** (geomembrane barriers isolate pollutants from groundwater).
- **Hydrocarbon-spill agricultural sites** where vertical polymer curtains prevent leachate migration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Percolation Reduction Efficiency from Darcy's Law

**Problem Statement:** A 1.0 hectare field has unlined percolation characterized by $K_u = 2.4 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. After installation of a 0.5 mm HDPE liner with $K_l = 1.0 \times 10^{-13}$ m/s, derive the percentage reduction in percolation assuming identical hydraulic gradient $i = 0.05$ for both cases.

**Step 1 — State Darcy's Law for the unlined (baseline) condition:**

$$
Q_u = K_u \cdot i \cdot A
$$

**Step 2 — State Darcy's Law for the lined (post-intervention) condition:**

$$
Q_l = K_l \cdot i \cdot A
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the unlined percolation rate (specific discharge) per unit area:**

$$
v_u = \frac{Q_u}{A} = K_u \cdot i
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
v_u = (2.4 \times 10^{-5}) \times 0.05
$$

$$
v_u = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the lined percolation rate per unit area:**

$$
v_l = K_l \cdot i
$$

$$
v_l = (1.0 \times 10^{-13}) \times 0.05
$$

$$
v_l = 5.0 \times 10^{-15} \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Percolation Reduction Efficiency (PRE) definition:**

$$
P_{\text{red}} = \frac{v_u - v_l}{v_u} \times 100
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the values and simplify (showing every algebraic step):**

$$
P_{\text{red}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-6} - 5.0 \times 10^{-15}}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}} \times 100
$$

**Step 7 — Recognize that the second term in the numerator is ~9 orders of magnitude smaller, hence negligible for engineering purposes:**

$$
P_{\text{red}} \approx \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}} \times 100
$$

$$
P_{\text{red}} \approx 99.9996 \%
$$

**Conclusion:** The HDPE liner reduces percolation by essentially **100%** (≈ 99.9996%), demonstrating the exceptional effectiveness of polymer films.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Water Saved Over an Irrigation Season

**Problem Statement:** A 0.5 ha vegetable field receives flood irrigation. Without a polymer mulch, the seasonal percolation loss is measured at $P_u = 250$ mm depth. With a 50 µm black LDPE mulch film, percolation is reduced to $P_l = 50$ mm. Compute the volume of water saved per season.

**Step 1 — Convert percolation depth to volume using the field area:**

$$
V = P \times A
$$

Where $A = 0.5$ ha = $0.5 \times 10{,}000$ m² = $5{,}000$ m².

**Step 2 — Compute unlined water loss:**

$$
V_u = P_u \times A = (0.250 \text{ m}) \times (5{,}000 \text{ m}^2)
$$

$$
V_u = 1{,}250 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Compute lined water loss:**

$$
V_l = P_l \times A = (0.050 \text{ m}) \times (5{,}000 \text{ m}^2)
$$

$$
V_l = 250 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Compute water saved (subtraction):**

$$
V_s = V_u - V_l = 1{,}250 - 250
$$

$$
V_s = 1{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per season}
$$

**Step 5 — Express in litres (multiply by 1000):**

$$
V_s = 1{,}000 \times 1{,}000 = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ litres}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 50 µm LDPE mulch film saves **1 million litres** of irrigation water per 0.5 ha per season — a critical volume in water-stressed KTU-relevant regions like Kerala's summer-paddy belts.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Superabsorbent Polymer (SAP) Water-Retention Capacity

**Problem Statement:** A farmer applies sodium polyacrylate SAP crystals at a rate of $M_{\text{SAP}} = 3$ kg/m² to a sandy loam root zone of depth 30 cm. The polymer absorption capacity is $C_{\text{abs}} = 400$ g water/g polymer, and the field-application efficiency is $\eta = 0.75$ (i.e., 25% loss to sub-root percolation and evaporation). Compute the additional plant-available water stored per square metre.

**Step 1 — Write the retention equation:**

$$
W_r = M_{\text{SAP}} \cdot C_{\text{abs}} \cdot \eta
$$

**Step 2 — Convert units to grams for consistency:**

$M_{\text{SAP}} = 3$ kg/m² = $3{,}000$ g/m², and $C_{\text{abs}} = 400$ g/g.

**Step 3 — Substitute the values:**

$$
W_r = 3{,}000 \text{ g/m}^2 \times 400 \text{ g/g} \times 0.75
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply stepwise:**

$$
W_r = 3{,}000 \times 400 \times 0.75
$$

$$
W_r = 1{,}200{,}000 \times 0.75
$$

$$
W_r = 900{,}000 \text{ g/m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to litres (1 litre of water ≈ 1000 g):**

$$
W_r = 900{,}000 \div 1{,}000 = 900 \text{ litres/m}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mm depth equivalent over the root zone (30 cm = 0.30 m = 0.30 m³ per m²):**

$$
\text{Additional water} = \frac{900 \text{ L/m}^2}{1{,}000 \text{ L/m}^3} = 0.900 \text{ m} = 900 \text{ mm depth of root zone}
$$

**Step 7 — Express as Available Water Capacity (AWC) increase per m²:**

The 30 cm root zone holds approximately 900 mm of additional water = **equivalent to ~ 3 times the field capacity** of untreated sandy loam.

**Conclusion:** SAP amendment at 3 kg/m² increases the plant-available water content by **900 L/m²**, dramatically reducing irrigation frequency and percolation loss.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Polymer Selection Guide

| Polymer Class | Trade Name Example | Best Application | Reduction in Percolation | Cost Index |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| HDPE Film (0.5 mm) | GSE HDPE | Pond/Canal Lining | 95–99% | Medium |
| LDPE Black Mulch (50 µm) | Sigma, Neoperth | Field row cover | 40–70% | Low |
| Sodium Polyacrylate | Aquasorb, Stockosorb | Soil amendment in root zone | 30–60% | High |
| Cross-linked Polyacrylamide | Soil Guard, APAM | Erosion + percolation control | 20–50% | Medium |
| PVC Geomembrane (1 mm) | Enviro Liner | Heavy-duty pond lining | 99%+ | Medium-High |
| EPDM Rubber Liner | Firestone EPDM | Water harvesting tanks | 98–99% | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Polymer-Soil-Water Interaction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Irrigation Water Input] --> B[Soil Surface Layer]
    B --> C{Macroporosity Check}
    C -- High Macropores --> D[Active Percolation Pathway]
    C -- Low Macropores --> E[Reduced Percolation]
    D --> F[Water Lost Below Root Zone]
    F --> G[Deep Groundwater Recharge]
    E --> H[Water Held in Root Zone]
    H --> I[Crop Uptake via Roots]
    I --> J[Biomass and Yield]
    K[Polymer Intervention System] --> K1[Type 1: Plastic Film Barrier]
    K --> K2[Type 2: Superabsorbent Polymer SAP]
    K --> K3[Type 3: Polyacrylamide Conditioner]
    K1 --> C
    K2 --> H
    K3 --> C
    J --> L[KTU Outcome Improved WUE and Yield]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: HDPE Liner Installation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Site Survey and Soil Testing] --> P2[Subgrade Compaction and Smoothing]
    P2 --> P3[Geotextile Underlay Placement]
    P3 --> P4[HDPE Liner Unrolling and Welding]
    P4 --> P5[Anchoring in Trench]
    P5 --> P6[Leak Testing and QC]
    P6 --> P7[Protective Soil Cover or Water Fill]
    P7 --> P8[Operational Farm Pond with Reduced Percolation]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparative Block Diagram: With vs Without Polymer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Without_Polymer
        W1[Surface Water Applied] --> W2[Soil Infiltration]
        W2 --> W3[Macropore Flow]
        W3 --> W4[Deep Percolation Loss]
        W4 --> W5[Groundwater Below Root Zone]
    end
    subgraph With_Polymer
        X1[Surface Water Applied] --> X2[Soil Infiltration]
        X2 --> X3[Polymer Film Barrier Blocks Flow]
        X3 --> X4[Water Stored in Root Zone]
        X4 --> X5[Crop Water Uptake]
    end
    W5 -.->|Comparison Result| Y[Net Loss: 30 to 60 Percent]
    X5 -.-> Y2[Net Loss: Reduced to 5 to 15 Percent]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define percolation loss in agriculture and explain how polymer films help to control it.**

**Model Answer:**
Percolation loss is the downward movement of applied irrigation water below the crop root zone into the deep subsoil, making it unavailable to plants. Polymer films (such as HDPE, LDPE, or PVC geomembranes) act as impermeable physical barriers with hydraulic conductivity in the order of $10^{-12}$ to $10^{-14}$ m/s. When laid at the sub-surface or as a bottom liner in canals/ponds, they block the gravitational flow of water, reducing percolation losses by 95% to 99% as dictated by Darcy's Law $Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$, where K is drastically reduced by the polymer film.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What are superabsorbent polymers (SAPs)? State two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
Superabsorbent polymers are cross-linked hydrophilic polymer networks capable of absorbing and retaining 300 to 1000 times their own weight in water. They function by hydrogen bonding water molecules to their functional groups (–COONa, –CONH₂), locking the water in a gel form that resists gravitational drainage. Two examples are **(i) Sodium polyacrylate** and **(ii) Cross-linked polyacrylamide**. They are mixed into the root zone soil at rates of 2 to 5 kg/m² to reduce percolation and increase water-holding capacity.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the different types of polymers used in the control of percolation losses in agricultural fields, with examples and mechanisms of action.

**Model Solution:**

Polymers used in percolation control fall into three functional categories, each exploiting a different physical-chemical mechanism.

**1. Plastic Films and Geo-membranes (Physical Barrier Mechanism)**
These are continuous impermeable sheets laid at the soil-water interface. They physically obstruct water flow.
- **HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene):** Most common pond/canal liner; thickness 0.5 to 2.0 mm; K ≈ $10^{-13}$ m/s; UV-stabilized; chemically inert.
- **LDPE (Low-Density Polyethylene):** Used as black/silver mulch film (25 to 100 µm); reduces surface evaporation and indirectly reduces percolation by controlling infiltration uniformity.
- **PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride):** Flexible geomembrane for canal lining; 0.75 to 1.5 mm; high puncture resistance.
- **EPDM (Ethylene Propylene Diene Monomer):** Synthetic rubber geomembrane for water harvesting tanks; high elasticity, very long service life.
- **Mechanism:** By Darcy's Law, the percolation flux $v = K \cdot i$ is proportional to the hydraulic conductivity of the boundary. Replacing soil (K ≈ $10^{-5}$ m/s) with a polymer film (K ≈ $10^{-13}$ m/s) reduces flux by 8 orders of magnitude.

**2. Superabsorbent Polymers (Chemical Retention Mechanism)**
These are cross-linked hydrogel-forming polymers that absorb and immobilize water.
- **Sodium Polyacrylate (SPA):** Most widely used SAP; absorption capacity 400 to 800 g/g; forms transparent gel.
- **Cross-linked Polyacrylamide (PAM):** Absorption 200 to 400 g/g; longer soil residence time.
- **Mechanism:** Hydrophilic functional groups along the polymer chain bond with water molecules, holding them in a 3D matrix with matric potential exceeding the gravitational potential. The water is thus not free to percolate.

**3. Polymer Soil Conditioners (Structural Modification Mechanism)**
These improve soil aggregation, reducing macroporosity.
- **Anionic Polyacrylamide (APAM):** Long-chain polymer; adsorbs onto clay particles; flocculates them into stable aggregates.
- **Hydrolyzed Polyacrylonitrile (HPAN):** Used in sandy soils.
- **Mechanism:** Flocculated aggregates create more mesopores (which hold plant-available water) and fewer macropores (which conduct gravitational drainage), thereby reducing percolation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 2.0 ha farm pond is to be lined with 1.0 mm HDPE geomembrane. The unlined soil has hydraulic conductivity $K_u = 3.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m/s. The HDPE liner has $K_l = 1.0 \times 10^{-13}$ m/s. If the average hydraulic gradient is 0.04, compute: (i) the percolation rate per unit area without and with lining, and (ii) the volume of water saved per day.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Percolation rate per unit area:**

Using Darcy's Law $v = K \cdot i$:

Without lining:
$$
v_u = K_u \cdot i = (3.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.04 = 1.2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m/s}
$$

With HDPE lining:
$$
v_l = K_l \cdot i = (1.0 \times 10^{-13}) \times 0.04 = 4.0 \times 10^{-15} \text{ m/s}
$$

**(ii) Volume of water saved per day:**

Area of pond: $A = 2.0$ ha = $2.0 \times 10{,}000 = 20{,}000$ m².

Time: $t = 1$ day = 86,400 s.

Water lost without lining per day:
$$
V_u = v_u \cdot A \cdot t = (1.2 \times 10^{-7}) \times (20{,}000) \times (86{,}400)
$$

$$
V_u = 1.2 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.728 \times 10^9 = 207.36 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

Water lost with lining per day (negligible):
$$
V_l = v_l \cdot A \cdot t \approx 6.91 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \approx 0
$$

Water saved per day:
$$
V_s = V_u - V_l \approx 207.36 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \approx 207{,}360 \text{ litres/day}
$$

**Conclusion:** The HDPE liner saves approximately **207 m³ of water per day** for a 2 ha pond — a substantial saving in irrigation economics.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of superabsorbent polymers (SAPs) in increasing water-use efficiency in sandy soils. Include their mechanism, application method, and limitations.

**Model Solution:**

**Role of SAPs in Sandy Soils:**
Sandy soils have large macropores, low organic matter, and poor water-holding capacity (typically 8–12% by weight). Irrigation water applied to sandy fields percolates rapidly below the root zone, often within hours. SAPs address this by acting as *in-situ water reservoirs* dispersed throughout the root zone.

**Mechanism of Action:**
1. SAP crystals absorb water during irrigation/rainfall, swelling into gel particles.
2. The absorbed water is held by *matric suction* greater than the gravitational potential driving percolation.
3. As the soil dries, plant roots extract water from the gel via osmotic gradient.
4. After depletion, SAPs re-swell when the next irrigation event occurs (reversible cycle).

**Application Method:**
- **Broadcast mixing:** Dry SAP crystals are mixed into the top 15–30 cm of soil at rates of 2 to 5 kg/m² before sowing.
- **Band placement:** SAP is placed in furrows near the root zone for row crops.
- **Seed coating:** SAP is coated directly onto seeds for germination-stage moisture insurance.
- **Hydrogel drench:** Pre-hydrated SAP gel is applied via fertigation.

**Limitations:**
1. **Cost:** SAPs are expensive (₹200–500/kg), limiting adoption in low-margin crops.
2. **Salt sensitivity:** Sodium polyacrylate loses 50–70% absorption in saline water (EC > 4 dS/m), a major issue in coastal Kerala and semi-arid zones.
3. **UV degradation:** Surface-applied SAP must be covered with soil or mulch.
4. **Limited lifespan:** Cross-linked SAPs degrade in 3–7 years; need reapplication.
5. **Over-hydration swelling:** Excessive application can heave the soil and damage shallow-rooted crops.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the additional water-holding capacity contributed by 4 kg/m² of a superabsorbent polymer (SAP) in a 30 cm root zone, given that the polymer's absorption capacity is 500 g/g and the field efficiency factor is 0.80.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $M_{\text{SAP}} = 4$ kg/m², $C_{\text{abs}} = 500$ g/g, $\eta = 0.80$, root zone depth = 30 cm.

**Step 1 — Convert SAP mass to grams:**
$$
M_{\text{SAP}} = 4 \times 1{,}000 = 4{,}000 \text{ g/m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the water-retention equation:**
$$
W_r = M_{\text{SAP}} \cdot C_{\text{abs}} \cdot \eta
$$

$$
W_r = 4{,}000 \times 500 \times 0.80
$$

**Step 3 — Compute stepwise:**
$$
W_r = 2{,}000{,}000 \times 0.80 = 1{,}600{,}000 \text{ g/m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to litres (1 L = 1000 g):**
$$
W_r = 1{,}600{,}000 \div 1{,}000 = 1{,}600 \text{ L/m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Express as equivalent depth of water in the 30 cm root zone:**

Root zone volume per m² = 0.30 m × 1 m² = 0.30 m³ = 300 L.

Additional water stored = 1,600 L/m² = **533%** of the original root zone water capacity.

**Step 6 — Convert to mm depth over the field:**
$$
\text{Depth} = \frac{1{,}600 \text{ L/m}^2}{10 \text{ L/m}^2/\text{mm}} = 160 \text{ mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** The SAP at 4 kg/m² contributes an additional **160 mm of plant-available water** in the 30 cm root zone — equivalent to more than half the typical weekly consumptive use of vegetable crops in summer.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency is critical:** Always convert kg ↔ g and ha ↔ m² before substituting. Most KTU students lose 1 to 2 marks per numerical on unit conversion errors.
> 2. **The "negligible" trap:** When $K_l$ is 8+ orders of magnitude smaller than $K_u$, do NOT skip the calculation; show the comparison step to earn full credit.
> 3. **Direction of $K$ reduction:** Many students mistakenly *increase* K when polymer is added. Remember: polymer films DECREASE hydraulic conductivity; SAPs DECREASE effective percolation flux.
> 4. **Table formatting:** Do not write the formula symbols in absolute-value notation using `|` inside markdown tables in your answer sheets — use brackets like $[ \, ]$ or braces.
> 5. **Distinguish mulching from lining:** Mulch films reduce *evaporation* and *weed transpiration*, only indirectly reducing percolation. Liner films *directly* block percolation. Examiners are strict on this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Percolation loss** = downward water loss below the effective root zone, classified as a *deep drainage* loss in irrigation efficiency calculations.
- **Darcy's Law** $Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$ is the foundational equation for all seepage/percolation analyses.
- **Hydraulic conductivity (K)** of polymer films ($10^{-12}$ to $10^{-14}$ m/s) is 8–10 orders lower than soil ($10^{-5}$ to $10^{-7}$ m/s), making them effective barriers.
- **Three polymer classes:**
   - *Films/geomembranes* (HDPE, LDPE, PVC, EPDM) → physical barrier.
   - *Superabsorbent polymers* (Sodium polyacrylate, cross-linked PAM) → chemical retention.
   - *Polymer conditioners* (Anionic PAM, HPAN) → soil structure modification.
- **Percolation Reduction Efficiency (PRE)** formula: $P_{\text{red}} = \dfrac{P_u - P_l}{P_u} \times 100$.
- **Water saved:** $V_s = (P_u - P_l) \times A \times t$ — apply unit conversion rigorously.
- **SAP water retention:** $W_r = M_{\text{SAP}} \cdot C_{\text{abs}} \cdot \eta$ — typical capacities range 300–1000 g/g.
- **Mulch films** reduce evaporation 50–90% and indirectly reduce percolation by improving infiltration uniformity.
- **Lined ponds/canals** achieve 95–99% reduction in seepage.
- **Limitations of SAPs:** cost, salt sensitivity, UV degradation, finite lifespan.
- **Application rates:** HDPE liner (0.5–2.0 mm thick), LDPE mulch (25–100 µm), SAPs (2–5 kg/m²).
- **KTU-recommended focus topics:** Darcy's Law derivations, lining material comparisons, numerical problems on PRE and water saved, SAP mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soil Conditioning, Soil Solarisation, and Effects of Different Colour Plastic Mulching in Surface Covered Cultivation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT611 - PLASTIC APPLICATION IN AGRICULTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Soil Conditioning, Soil Solarisation, and Effects of Different Colour Plastic Mulching in Surface Covered Cultivation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Soil Conditioning (in Plasticulture Context)

**Formal Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soil Conditioning** in plasticulture refers to the pre-planting treatment of soil using plasticulture techniques (films, mulches, fumigation films, drainage pipes) to modify the physical, chemical, and biological properties of the soil—improving its structure, moisture retention, aeration, salinity control, and microbial balance—to create a more productive and stable root-zone environment for crops.

In the **KTU 2024 syllabus** context, soil conditioning through plastics includes the use of:
- **Plastic mulches** (to regulate temperature and moisture)
- **Polyethylene fumigation films** (to trap fumigant gases like methyl bromide, chloropicrin)
- **Subsurface drainage pipes** (PVC/HDPE laterals) for salinity and water-table control
- **Plastic-lined channels/ponds** for preventing seepage losses

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of soil conditioning like preparing a bed before sleeping. You fluff the pillow, tighten the sheets, and adjust the room temperature so your body can rest properly. Similarly, before planting, we "fluff" the soil—breaking clods, balancing moisture, removing salts, and killing pathogens—so the plant's roots can "sleep" (grow) in comfort. Plastics act like the **fitted bedsheet** that holds the condition you created.

**Standard Metrics in Soil Conditioning:**
- **Bulk Density (ρ_b):** 1.1 – 1.6 g/cm³ (ideal for arable soil: **< 1.4 g/cm³**)
- **Soil Porosity:** **40–60 %** (optimal root zone)
- **EC (Electrical Conductivity):** **< 4 dS/m** for most crops
- **Soil pH:** **6.0 – 7.5** for most arable crops
- **Organic Carbon:** **> 0.5 %** in tropical Indian soils

---

## 1.2 Soil Solarisation

**Formal Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soil Solarisation** is a non-chemical, environment-friendly hydrothermal disinfestation method in which moist soil is covered with thin, transparent polyethylene film (typically 25–50 µm) for 4–6 weeks during the hot summer months (April–June in India). The film traps incoming solar radiation, raising the soil temperature to lethal levels (40–55 °C at 5 cm depth; up to 60–70 °C at the surface) for soil-borne pathogens, weed seeds, nematodes, and insects.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine sealing a car parked in the sun on a hot summer day with all windows closed. The temperature inside becomes unbearable. Soil solarisation works the same way—the transparent film acts as a "greenhouse roof" sealing the soil. Sunlight enters, gets absorbed by the dark soil surface, becomes heat, but cannot escape back as long-wave radiation (greenhouse effect). The soil literally "cooks" its own pests out.

**Mechanism Mnemonic — "STEM":**
- **S**olar radiation enters through transparent film
- **T**rapped heat raises soil temperature (10–15 °C above ambient)
- **E**levated temperature + moisture → kills pathogens, weeds, nematodes
- **M**aintained for 4–6 weeks → complete disinfestation

**Key Solarisation Parameters:**
- **Film thickness:** 25–50 µm (LDP/LLDPE)
- **Film color:** Transparent (NOT black)
- **Duration:** 4–6 weeks
- **Soil moisture:** Field capacity (to make pests turgid and vulnerable)
- **Best season:** Peak summer (April–June in Kerala/Most of India)
- **Temperature achieved:** 50–60 °C at 5 cm; 40–45 °C at 30 cm

---

## 1.3 Plastic Mulching (Surface Covered Cultivation)

**Formal Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plastic Mulching** is the practice of covering the soil surface around cultivated plants with a thin plastic film (50–200 µm) to modify the soil microclimate, conserve moisture, suppress weeds, regulate temperature, prevent erosion, and in some cases alter the spectral quality of light reaching the plant canopy. Different colored mulches produce distinctly different agronomic effects, and selecting the right color is a key KTU high-yield topic.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a colored plastic mulch as a **reflective jacket worn by the soil**. A black jacket (black mulch) absorbs sunlight and warms the soil. A white jacket (white mulch) reflects sunlight and keeps the soil cool. A silver jacket (silver mulch) reflects specific UV wavelengths that confuse insect pests. The plant "sees" different light environments depending on the color of the mulch beneath it.

**The Six Major Colored Mulches (KTU High-Yield):**

| Color | Key Effect | Best Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Black** | Blocks light → kills weeds; warms soil 2–4 °C | General purpose, summer |
| **Transparent** | Maximum soil warming (5–8 °C); poor weed control | Soil solarisation |
| **Silver/Reflective** | Repels aphids, whiteflies; UV reflection | Pest management |
| **Red** | Reflects far-red; boosts tomato, strawberry yield | Fruiting crops |
| **Blue** | Enhances vegetative growth | Cucurbits, some ornamentals |
| **White** | Cools soil 2–3 °C; reflects light | Hot climates, lettuce |
| **Yellow** | Attracts aphids/whiteflies (sticky trap use) | IPM |
---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Soil Conditioning — Theoretical Framework

Soil conditioning in plasticulture is governed by the **Soil–Water–Plant (SWP) continuum**. The interventions target three sub-systems:

### A. Physical Conditioning
- Plastic mulches reduce **soil crust formation** by intercepting raindrop impact.
- Reduces **bulk density** in the top 0–15 cm layer by **10–15 %**.
- Improves **aggregate stability** (% water-stable aggregates > 0.25 mm).

### B. Chemical Conditioning
- Plastic fumigation films trap volatile fumigants, increasing their residence time from hours to days.
- **Lateral HDPE/PVC drainage pipes** (typically 50–80 mm diameter, laid at 60–100 cm depth) leach down excess salts:
  - **Drainage coefficient (Dc)** follows Hooghoudt's equation (for steady-state):

$$
q = \frac{4 K L \left( h^2 - d^2 \right)}{S^2}
$$

where:
- $q$ = drainage discharge rate (m/day)
- $K$ = hydraulic conductivity (m/day)
- $L$ = drain spacing (m)
- $h$ = midpoint water table height above drain (m)
- $d$ = equivalent depth (m)
- $S$ = drain spacing (m)

### C. Biological Conditioning
- Mulches create a **"moisture–temperature refuge"** for beneficial microbes.
- Solarisation shifts microbial community toward thermophilic, often beneficial populations.

---

## 2.2 Soil Solarisation — Step-by-Step Mechanism

### Step 1: Soil Preparation
- Deep plough (25–30 cm) to expose sub-soil pests and break hardpan.
- Remove crop residues, stones, and clods (> 5 cm) to ensure film–soil contact.
- Apply compost/FYM (10–25 t/ha) — this amplifies the biocidal effect because **volatile compounds released by heating organic matter** are themselves toxic to pathogens.

### Step 2: Irrigation to Field Capacity
- Flood or sprinkler-irrigate to bring soil to **field capacity** (matric potential ≈ -0.03 MPa or pF ≈ 1.8).
- **Why?** Moist soil conducts heat **3–4× faster** than dry soil. Water also makes nematode eggs, fungal spores, and weed seeds **metabolically active and turgid**, making them thermally vulnerable.

### Step 3: Film Laying
- Use **25–50 µm LLDPE transparent film**.
- Seal edges with soil (15–20 cm deep furrow filled back).
- The film must be **intact, taut, and in close contact with soil surface** (no air gap, or convection cools the soil).

### Step 4: Solar Exposure
- Expose for **4–6 weeks** during peak summer.
- Solar radiation flux: **600–900 W/m²** at midday in Indian plains.
- Soil temperature profile achieved:

$$
T_{soil}(z, t) = T_{avg} + A \cdot e^{-z/d} \cdot \sin(\omega t - z/d)
$$

where:
- $T_{soil}(z,t)$ = soil temperature at depth $z$ and time $t$
- $T_{avg}$ = annual mean surface temperature
- $A$ = annual temperature amplitude
- $d$ = **damping depth** (m) = $\sqrt{2K/\omega C}$
- $\omega$ = angular frequency ($2\pi/365$ days)
- $K$ = thermal conductivity; $C$ = volumetric heat capacity

> **Simplified for summer period:** Temperature drops exponentially with depth. A typical damping depth $d$ for moist soil is **0.10–0.20 m**.

### Step 5: Removal & Planting
- After 4–6 weeks, remove film, lightly cultivate, and transplant or sow the main crop.

---

## 2.3 Effects of Different Coloured Plastic Mulches — The "Spectrum of Effects"

The agronomic effect of a coloured mulch is governed by three optical properties:

$$
\text{Effect} \;=\; f(\alpha, \rho, \tau)
$$

- $\alpha$ = **Absorptivity** (fraction of incident radiation absorbed)
- $\rho$ = **Reflectivity** (fraction reflected)
- $\tau$ = **Transmissivity** (fraction transmitted through film)

Conservation law:

$$
\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pearl:** The color of a mulch determines the **ratio of energy reflected upward (toward plant canopy) vs. downward (into soil)**. This is the central concept behind why color choice matters.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation / Statement | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Energy balance on mulch | $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ | Dimensionless |
| Soil heat flux (Fourier) | $q_z = -K \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial z}$ | W/m² |
| Thermal diffusivity | $\kappa = \dfrac{K}{\rho c_p}$ | m²/s (soil: ~1×10⁻⁷) |
| Damping depth | $d = \sqrt{2\kappa / \omega}$ | m (soil: 0.1–0.2 m) |
| Black mulch warming | $\Delta T_{5cm} \approx +2 \text{ to } +4 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | vs. bare soil |
| Transparent mulch warming | $\Delta T_{5cm} \approx +5 \text{ to } +8 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | vs. bare soil |
| White mulch cooling | $\Delta T_{5cm} \approx -2 \text{ to } -3 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | vs. bare soil |
| Silver mulch pest reduction | Aphid landing reduced **60–80 %** | vs. bare soil |
| Red mulch tomato yield gain | **+12 to +20 %** marketable yield | vs. black mulch |
| Solarisation duration | 4–6 weeks | peak summer |
| Solarisation film thickness | 25–50 µm | LDP/LLDPE transparent |
| Mulch film thickness | 50–200 µm (25 µm for solarisation) | LDPE/LLDPE |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Agricultural Utility

- **Soil conditioning** under HDPE mulched beds in Kerala's banana and vegetable systems has reduced irrigation water use by **30–45 %**.
- **Soil solarisation** is used in **polyhouse cut-flower cultivation** (rose, carnation) to sterilise beds without methyl bromide (now banned under Montreal Protocol).
- **Coloured mulches** in **strawberry** (red mulch) and **tomato** (silver/red mulch) are now standard in **Precision Farming Development Centres (PFDCs)** across India.
- The **NHM (National Horticulture Mission)** and **MIDH** schemes provide subsidies for plastic mulch in India.
---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Soil Temperature Under a Transparent Solarisation Film

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Treatment

**Setting:** Steady-state energy balance on the soil surface covered by transparent film.

**Step 1: Net radiation reaching soil**

The transparent film has transmissivity $\tau \approx 0.85$–$0.92$ in the shortwave band. Net shortwave radiation reaching soil:

$$
R_{sw,\,soil} = \tau \cdot R_{sw,\,inc}
$$

For $R_{sw,\,inc} = 800 \text{ W/m}^2$ (typical peak summer noon in Kerala):

$$
R_{sw,\,soil} = 0.88 \times 800 = 704 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Energy balance at soil surface**

$$
R_{sw,\,soil} - R_{lw,\,soil} - H - LE - G = 0
$$

where:
- $R_{lw,\,soil}$ = longwave back-radiation from soil
- $H$ = sensible heat flux to air
- $LE$ = latent heat flux (evaporation)
- $G$ = soil heat flux (downward)

Under the film, $H$ and $LE$ are drastically reduced (film blocks turbulent exchange), so **most energy goes into $G$**.

**Step 3: Estimate soil heat flux**

Assume only 5 % loss to $H$ + $LE$ combined (typical for sealed film):

$$
G \approx 0.95 \times R_{sw,\,soil} = 0.95 \times 704 = 668.8 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Conduction into soil (1-D Fourier)**

$$
G = -K \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}
$$

For moist soil, $K \approx 1.0 \text{ W/(m·K)}$. Solving for surface temperature rise at steady state at depth $z = 0.05 \text{ m}$:

$$
\Delta T(0.05) \approx \frac{G \cdot z}{K} = \frac{668.8 \times 0.05}{1.0} = 33.4 \,^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 5: Add ambient baseline**

Ambient soil temperature at 5 cm in May (Kerala) ≈ 32 °C:

$$
T_{5cm} = 32 + 33.4 \approx 65.4 \,^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

This explains why **transparent film under solarisation can push 5 cm soil temperature above 60 °C** — well above the **thermal death point** of most pathogens:

| Organism | Thermal Death Point (10–30 min exposure) |
|---|---|
| *Fusarium oxysporum* | 50–55 °C |
| *Verticillium dahliae* | 50 °C |
| *Phytophthora* spp. | 45–50 °C |
| Root-knot nematode (*Meloidogyne*) | 48–52 °C |
| Weed seeds (general) | 60–70 °C |

> **Conclusion of derivation:** A transparent film + 4–6 weeks of exposure can sterilise the top 0–20 cm of moist soil without chemicals.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Black Mulch Warms Soil Less Than Transparent Mulch

**Energy balance for black mulch ($\alpha \approx 0.95, \tau \approx 0$):**

Almost all incident shortwave radiation is absorbed by the black film.

**Problem:** Much of this energy is **re-radiated as longwave (LW)** back upward:

$$
R_{lw} = \varepsilon \sigma T_s^4
$$

The black film surface temperature can exceed 50–60 °C; most of this heat is lost to air by convection, not conducted downward.

**Result:**
- Black mulch: 2–4 °C rise at 5 cm.
- Transparent mulch: 5–8 °C rise at 5 cm (more energy reaches soil directly).

**For white mulch ($\rho \approx 0.6$):**

Most energy is reflected, so **net soil warming is negative** or near zero. Hence white mulch is used in **hot arid seasons** to **cool the root zone**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Energy Conservation Across a Mulch (Sanity Check)

For a silver-reflective mulch with $\alpha = 0.20$, $\rho = 0.75$, $\tau = 0.05$:

$$
\alpha + \rho + \tau = 0.20 + 0.75 + 0.05 = 1.00 \;\; \checkmark
$$

This confirms the **law of conservation of energy** at the film surface and explains why **silver mulches** are powerful **insect repellents** — they reflect **UV-A (320–400 nm)** which disorients aphids, thrips, and whiteflies, reducing virus-vector landings by 60–80 %.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Color-wise Performance Summary

| Property | Black | Transparent | Silver | Red | White | Blue |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ (absorptivity) | 0.90–0.95 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.40 | 0.30 | 0.45 |
| $\rho$ (reflectivity) | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.75 | 0.30 | 0.60 | 0.30 |
| $\tau$ (transmissivity) | 0.00 | 0.80 | 0.05 | 0.30 | 0.10 | 0.25 |
| Soil temp change (5 cm) | +2 to +4 °C | +5 to +8 °C | +1 °C | +2 °C | -2 to -3 °C | +1 °C |
| Weed control | Excellent | Poor | Good | Fair | Good | Fair |
| Insect repellent | No | No | Yes (UV) | Mild | No | No |
| Fruit yield effect | Neutral | Negative (weed competition) | +10 to +15 % | +12 to +20 % (tomato) | +5–8 % (cool season) | +5–10 % (cucurbits) |
| Best crop use | General vegetable | Soil solarisation | Tomato, capsicum, cucurbits | Tomato, strawberry | Lettuce, cole crops | Cucumber, melon |
| Film thickness (µm) | 25–50 | 25–50 | 25–50 | 25–50 | 25–50 | 25–50 |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Soil Solarisation — System Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Deep Ploughing and Removal of Clods] --> B[Step 2: Add FYM or Compost 10 to 25 tonnes per hectare]
    B --> C[Step 3: Flood Irrigate to Field Capacity]
    C --> D[Step 4: Lay 25 to 50 micrometre Transparent LLDPE Film]
    D --> E[Step 5: Seal Edges with 15 to 20 cm Soil Furrow]
    E --> F[Step 6: Expose to Sun for 4 to 6 Weeks Peak Summer]
    F --> G[Soil Temperatures: 50 to 60 C at 5 cm Depth]
    G --> H[Kills Pathogens, Nematodes, Weed Seeds, Insects]
    H --> I[Step 7: Remove Film, Lightly Cultivate, Plant Main Crop]
    I --> J[Outcome: Clean Seedbed with Reduced Pesticide Need]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incident Solar Radiation] --> B[Mulch Film Surface]
    B --> C{Absorbed by Film}
    B --> D{Reflected Upward to Canopy}
    B --> E{Transmitted Downward to Soil}
    C --> F[Convective and Longwave Loss to Air]
    C --> G[Conduction to Soil Increases Soil Temperature]
    D --> H[Modifies Light Quality Reaching Lower Leaves]
    D --> I[UV Reflection Repels Insect Pests]
    E --> J[Warms Soil and Conserves Moisture]
    G --> K[Weed Seeds Cannot Germinate in Dark for Black Mulch]
    J --> L[Root Zone Microclimate Improved]
    I --> M[Reduced Aphid and Whitefly Landing]
    H --> N[Altered Phytochrome Response and Yield]
    L --> O[Crop Growth and Yield Enhancement]
    M --> O
    K --> O
    N --> O
```

```mermaid
    Start[Choose Plastic Mulch Color] --> Q1{Primary Goal?}
    Q1 -->|Weed control + warming| Q2[Use Black Mulch]

    Q1 -->|Insect repellent| Q4[Use Silver Reflective Mulch]
    Q1 -->|Cool root zone| Q5[Use White Mulch]
    Q1 -->|Boost fruiting yield| Q6[Use Red Mulch]
    Q1 -->|Boost vegetative growth cucurbits| Q7[Use Blue Mulch]
    Q2 --> R1[Crops: Tomato, Capsicum, Brinjal, Okra]
    Q3 --> R2[Use: Soil Solarisation Only]
    Q4 --> R3[Crops: Tomato, Capsicum, Cucumber]
    Q5 --> R4[Crops: Lettuce, Cabbage, Cauliflower in Summer]

```

```mermaid
    BC --> B2[Promotes Beneficial Microbes]
    BC --> B3[Adds Organic Matter via Mulch Degradation]
    P1 --> OUT[Improved Root Zone Health]
```
