Was the trainee satisfied?"] lev2["LEVEL 2 LEARNING
Did the trainee gain knowledge and skill?"] lev3["LEVEL 3 BEHAVIOR
Did the trainee apply it on the farm?"] lev4["LEVEL 4 RESULTS
Did yield income and water saving improve?"] lev1 --> lev2 lev2 --> lev3 lev3 --> lev4 lev4 --> rev["REVISION OF MODULE AND RETURN TO LEVEL 1"] rev --> lev1 ``` **Why this matters in KTU:** The most common *valuation trap* in KTU answers is that students explain Level 1 (satisfaction) and Level 2 (learning) but forget Levels 3 and 4. Always end your HRD answer with **Level 4 — tangible results in ₹ or kg/ha**. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Q1. Define Human Resource Development (HRD) in the context of plasticulture. List any four target groups of HRD in plasticulture.** **Model Answer:** Human Resource Development (HRD) in plasticulture is the systematic process of enhancing the knowledge, skills, and attitude (KSA) of all stakeholders involved in the plasticulture value chain, from design and installation to operation, maintenance, and end-of-life recycling of agricultural plastics. The four primary target groups are: 1. **Farmers** (end-users of mulch, drip systems, polyhouses). 2. **Rural women and SHG members** (especially in vermi-beds, silage, and small polyhouse operations). 3. **Technicians and installers** (drip fitters, polyhouse erectors, welders). 4. **Extension officers and agricultural graduates** (the multiplier force). --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q2. What is the Knowledge Gain Index (KGI)? Write the formula and state the benchmark value for an "effective" training program.** **Model Answer:** The Knowledge Gain Index (KGI) is a normalized measure of the actual knowledge gained by a trainee relative to the maximum possible gain achievable during a training program. $$ KGI = \dfrac{P_{post} - P_{pre}}{T - P_{pre}} \times 100 $$ where $P_{pre}$ = pre-test score, $P_{post}$ = post-test score, and $T$ = maximum possible score. The **ICAR benchmark** for an "effective" agricultural training program is a **KGI ≥ 70%**. A KGI below 30% indicates a poor training design requiring urgent revision. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Model Paper Pattern]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the Knowledge–Skill–Attitude (KSA) framework in detail, giving **two specific examples** for each domain from the field of plasticulture. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A district agricultural officer conducts a 5-day drip irrigation training for 50 farmers. The pre-test average score is 20 out of 50 and the post-test average is 40 out of 50. Calculate the **Knowledge Gain Index (KGI)** and comment on the effectiveness of the training. The total cost of the training is ₹1,50,000 and the average additional income per farmer is ₹6,000 in the subsequent season. Calculate the **Return on Training Investment (RoTI)**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** The **KSA framework** is the foundational HRD model in plasticulture extension. - **Knowledge (Cognitive) — "WHY":** Example 1: Explaining *why* a 25-micron transparent polyethylene mulch raises soil temperature by 3–5 °C (the greenhouse effect of the film and reduced evaporative cooling). Example 2: Explaining *why* drip irrigation saves 30–50% water compared to flood irrigation (water is delivered at the root zone, eliminating conveyance and evaporation losses). - **Skill (Psychomotor) — "HOW":** Example 1: Demonstrating the *correct* procedure to lay a drip lateral with emitter spacing of 30 cm for row crops and 50 cm for wide-spaced crops like banana. Example 2: Practicing the *correct* technique to weld two sheets of 200-micron UV-stabilized polyhouse film using a hot-air gun at 280–320 °C without burning the film. - **Attitude (Affective) — "MINDSET":** Example 1: Cultivating the mindset to *flush the drip system* once a week to prevent emitter clogging (a habit, not just knowledge). Example 2: Cultivating the mindset to *collect and send end-of-life mulch film* to a registered recycler instead of burning it in the open field, in compliance with the **Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 (amended 2022)**. **Model Solution for (b):** Given: $P_{pre} = 20$, $P_{post} = 40$, $T = 50$, $N = 50$ farmers, $C = 1,50,000$ ₹, Extra income per farmer = ₹6,000. **Step 1 — Calculate KGI:** $$ \begin{aligned} KGI &= \dfrac{P_{post} - P_{pre}}{T - P_{pre}} \times 100 \\ KGI &= \dfrac{40 - 20}{50 - 20} \times 100 \\ KGI &= \dfrac{20}{30} \times 100 \\ KGI &= 66.67\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Comment on Effectiveness:** A KGI of **66.67%** is *good* but falls just short of the ICAR benchmark of 70% for "effective" training. The training design is sound but needs minor improvement — for example, more hands-on field practice, visual demonstrations, and a follow-up refresher after one month. **Step 3 — Calculate RoTI:** $$ \begin{aligned} B &= N \times \text{Extra income per farmer} \\ B &= 50 \times 6{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\ C &= 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\ RoTI &= \dfrac{B - C}{C} \times 100 \\ RoTI &= \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} \times 100 \\ RoTI &= \dfrac{1{,}50{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} \times 100 \\ RoTI &= 100\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** A RoTI of **100%** means the training has recovered its entire cost and generated an additional ₹1.5 lakh of social benefit. By KTU/ICAR standards, this is a **viable and justifiable** training program. The RoTI would significantly improve if the trainee count were increased (economies of scale). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Describe the **four levels of Kirkpatrick's training evaluation model** as applied to a mulching training program for vegetable growers. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **role of KVK, CIPET, NCPAH, and industry** in the human resource development of plasticulture in India. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** Kirkpatrick's model evaluates any training program across four progressive levels: - **Level 1 — Reaction:** Measures the *immediate satisfaction* of the farmer with the mulching training. Captured through feedback forms, smiling sheets, and verbal response at the end of the program. High satisfaction is necessary but **not sufficient** for learning. - **Level 2 — Learning:** Measures the *knowledge and skill acquired*. Administered through a pre-test / post-test (KGI calculation) and a practical assessment (the farmer must lay a 10 m² mulch bed correctly on a graded plot). - **Level 3 — Behavior:** Measures *on-farm application* of the skill 3 to 6 months later. For mulching, this is verified by farm visits — is the farmer actually using 25–50 micron mulch? Are the beds raised 15 cm? Are the edges properly tucked? - **Level 4 — Results:** Measures *tangible impact* on yield, water saving, income, and weed-control economics. For mulched tomato, yield increase of 30–40% and water saving of 25–35% is the typical result. Converting these into ₹/ha establishes the RoTI. **Model Solution for (b):** - **Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVF / KVK):** District-level farm science centres under ICAR that conduct **on-farm testing (OFT)**, **front-line demonstrations (FLD)**, and **short-term training** for farmers. KVKs are the *last-mile HRD delivery vehicle* for plasticulture in India, with 731 KVKs covering almost every district. - **Central Institute of Plastics Engineering and Technology (CIPET):** Specializes in **polymer science, plastic processing, and recycling**. CIPET conducts long-term (6-month to 2-year) diploma courses and short-term certificate programs for **technicians, entrepreneurs, and unemployed youth**, producing a skilled workforce for HDPE/LDPE film extrusion, injection moulding of emitters, and plastic recycling. - **National Committee on Plasticulture Applications in Horticulture (NCPAH):** An **apex coordinating body** under the Ministry of Agriculture that links R&D institutions, industry, state governments, and farmers. NCPAH designs HRD policy, prepares training manuals, and conducts national-level **Training of Trainers (TOT)** programs that cascade down to the field. - **Industry (e.g., Netafim, Jain Irrigation, Godrej Agrovet, Mahindra EPC):** Provides **product-specific hands-on training**, dealer education, and farmer demos. Industry-led HRD is critical for the latest technology transfer (e.g., sub-surface drip, anti-aphid nets, smart polyhouse automation) but is *commercially motivated* and therefore best **complemented** by the neutral inputs of KVK and CIPET. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls** > 1. **Writing generic HRD theory without plasticulture examples:** A 7-mark question on KSA *must* contain at least one specific plasticulture example per domain (mulch, drip, polyhouse, silage). Generic education theory scores 2–3 marks; plasticulture-specific answers score 6–7 marks. > 2. **Forgetting units and benchmark values:** Always write the unit (%, ₹/ha, kg/ha) and compare your numerical answer with the **ICAR / KTU benchmark** (e.g., KGI ≥ 70%, RoTI ≥ 100%, LER ≥ 1.5). > 3. **Omitting the feedback loop:** Any HRD answer without a "monitoring and evaluation" or "feedback" component is considered *incomplete* by KTU evaluators. Always close the loop. > 4. **Confusing HRD with extension:** HRD is a broader term encompassing training + education + skill development + capacity building. Extension is one *delivery mode* within HRD. > 5. **Ignoring women and SHGs:** Kerala's KTU pattern awards bonus marks for mentioning **gender-sensitive HRD**, such as women-led polyhouse SHGs in Wayanad or Idukki. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Definition:** HRD in plasticulture is the systematic development of **Knowledge + Skill + Attitude** of all stakeholders. - **Five Pillars:** Need Assessment → Curriculum Design → Delivery → M&E → Feedback. - **Key Formulas (must memorize):** KGI, RoTI, TCR, AR, Kirkpatrick L1–L4, Rogers' Logistic Curve $A(t) = K / (1 + e^{-b K t})$. - **Benchmark Values:** KGI ≥ 70%, RoTI ≥ 100%, TCR ≥ 60%, Trainer:Trainee = 1:25 (theory) / 1:10 (practical). - **Key Indian Institutions:** ICAR + KVK (extension & research), CIPET (polymer skill), NCPAH (apex coordination), SAUs (TNAU, IARI, KAU, MPKV) (curriculum). - **Government Schemes with HRD Component:** PMKSY-PDMC, MIDH, NMSA, PMKVY, DDU-GKY, ATMA. - **Special Focus (Kerala Context):** KAU-KVKs, VFPCK (Vegetable and Fruit Promotion Council, Kerala), Kudumbashree SHGs in polyhouse vegetable cultivation. - **Sustainability Link:** HRD must include training on **end-of-life plastic management** to comply with PWM Rules 2016 (amended 2022) and Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR). - **Adoption Barrier:** Lack of trained manpower is the **#1 non-monetary barrier** to plasticulture adoption in India — so HRD is as critical as subsidy. - **Digital HRD:** Kisan Call Centre (1551), ICAR e-Krishi Shiksha, WhatsApp-based FFS, YouTube agri-channels. - **Future Direction:** AI-driven personalized learning, AR/VR polyhouse simulators, blockchain-based skill certification.

topics.title: Water Management: Use of Plastics in In-Situ Moisture Conservation and Rain Water Harvesting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT611 - PLASTIC APPLICATION IN AGRICULTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Water Management: Use of Plastics in In-Situ Moisture Conservation and Rain Water Harvesting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 In-Situ Moisture Conservation
**In-situ moisture conservation** refers to a set of agronomic and engineering practices aimed at retaining rainfall water *where it falls* — directly at the root zone of crops — thereby minimizing surface runoff, deep percolation, and evaporative losses. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this is achieved primarily through the strategic deployment of **polymeric films and barriers** (plastic mulches, sub-surface barriers, and cover films).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Module 4):**
> *In-situ moisture conservation through plastics is the application of synthetic polymer films (LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE, PVC, EVA) on the soil surface or within the soil profile to intercept solar radiation, suppress evaporation, moderate soil temperature, and redirect water flow — thereby maximizing the fraction of incident rainfall that becomes plant-available water.*

### 1.2 Rain Water Harvesting (RWH)
**Rain Water Harvesting** is the collection, conveyance, and storage of runoff generated from precipitation events for productive use (irrigation, livestock, groundwater recharge). Plastics contribute to RWH through **geomembranes, pond liners, storage tanks, gutters, catchments, and conveyance pipes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Module 4):**
> *Rain water harvesting using plastics is the engineered capture and storage of precipitation using synthetic polymer-based infrastructure — including HDPE/LLDPE-lined farm ponds, Ferro-cement tanks with plastic liners, plastic roof catchments, PVC/MDPE conveyance networks, and geosynthetic-reinforced recharge structures — to bridge dry spells and augment water security.*

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

> [!TIP]
> **Think of plastic films as a "raincoat" for the soil:**
> - When you wear a raincoat, water either slides off (runoff) or stays on the outer surface (evaporation suppressed). The soil underneath stays dry and warm.
> - Similarly, a **plastic mulch film** acts as a raincoat for the soil — it blocks direct solar radiation (reducing evaporation by 50–70 %), reflects or transmits specific wavelengths (controlling weed germination), and channels rainwater to the plant root zone.
> - In **rain water harvesting**, the pond liner acts like a **bathtub liner** — without it, water seeps into the ground and is lost; with it, the water is held for use, just like a bath tub holds water until you are ready to use it.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value / Standard | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Plastic mulch film thickness | **25 – 100 µm** (most common: 25 – 50 µm) | Microns |
| HDPE pond liner thickness | **200 – 1500 µm (0.2 – 1.5 mm)** | mm |
| UV-stabilized LDPE life | **3 – 5 years** (field) | Years |
| Mulch film width | **0.9 m, 1.2 m, 1.5 m** | m |
| Color of mulch | **Black, Silver-black, Transparent, White** | — |
| Evaporation reduction (with mulch) | **40 – 70 %** | % |
| Soil temperature gain (transparent mulch) | **+2 to +5 °C** | °C |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Plastic Use in Water Management
Water is the most limiting factor in Indian agriculture (~80 % of freshwater is consumed by agriculture). The two principal pathways of water loss are:

1. **Evaporation** from bare soil (accounts for 30 – 50 % of total ET in arid zones).
2. **Seepage / percolation** from unlined storage structures (losses of 30 – 60 % in earthen ponds).

Plastics intervene at **both** stages using scientifically validated mechanisms.

### 2.2 Mechanism of Plastic Mulch in Moisture Conservation (Step-by-Step)

1. **Interception of solar radiation:** The opaque (black/silver) film prevents photons from reaching the soil surface, eliminating the energy required to vaporize water.
2. **Vapor barrier formation:** A thin, saturated air layer forms just above the film; vapor pressure equilibrium drastically reduces diffusive loss.
3. **Soil temperature moderation:** Transparent films transmit short-wave radiation and trap long-wave radiation → greenhouse effect under film.
4. **Weed suppression:** Opaque films block photosynthetically active radiation (PAR) → no weed germination → no transpiration competition.
5. **Runoff redirection:** Raised-bed + mulch channels lateral flow of excess rain to furrows and root zone.

### 2.3 Mechanism of Plastic Liners in Rain Water Harvesting

1. **Impermeability:** HDPE has a hydraulic conductivity of **~10⁻¹³ cm/s** — effectively zero seepage.
2. **Chemical inertness:** Resistant to acidic/alkaline soil, no biodegradation for 20+ years when UV-stabilized.
3. **Mechanical robustness:** Tensile strength **> 27 MPa** (HDPE), puncture resistance sufficient for sub-grade.
4. **Weldability:** Sheets can be thermally welded on-site to form seamless large-area liners.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for KTU 2024 ESE — they appear in both Part A derivations and Part B numericals.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{\text{red}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{open}} - E_{\text{covered}}}{E_{\text{open}}} \times 100$ | Evaporation reduction efficiency | % |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{mulch}} = \dfrac{WUE_{\text{mulched}}}{WUE_{\text{bare}}}$ | Relative Water Use Efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $WUE = \dfrac{Y}{ET_c}$ | Water Use Efficiency (Yield per unit ET) | kg·ha⁻¹·mm⁻¹ |
| 4 | $V_{\text{pond}} = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot h \cdot (A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 \cdot A_2})$ | Volume of trapezoidal farm pond (Prismoidal) | m³ |
| 5 | $V_{\text{simple}} = A \cdot d$ | Volume of rectangular/cylindrical tank | m³ |
| 6 | $Q_{\text{harvest}} = P \cdot A \cdot C \cdot \eta_s$ | Annual harvested runoff volume | m³/year |
| 7 | $C = \dfrac{R}{P}$ | Runoff coefficient (ratio of runoff to rainfall) | Dimensionless (0 – 1) |
| 8 | $S_{\text{seepage}} = K \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dx} \cdot t$ | Darcy's seepage loss (K = hydraulic conductivity) | m³ |
| 9 | $S_{\text{evap}} = E_{\text{pan}} \cdot K_p \cdot A_s \cdot t$ | Pan-evaporation based storage loss | m³ |
| 10 | $S_{\text{net}} = P \cdot A \cdot C - S_{\text{seepage}} - S_{\text{evap}} - O_{\text{overflow}}$ | Net annual water available | m³/year |

> Where:
> - $P$ = annual precipitation (m or mm)
> - $A$ = catchment area (m²)
> - $C$ = runoff coefficient (asphalt: 0.85, RCC roof: 0.80, soil: 0.30)
> - $\eta_s$ = system efficiency (typically 0.80 – 0.90)
> - $K$ = hydraulic conductivity (m/s)
> - $K_p$ = pan coefficient (0.70 – 0.80 for Class-A pan)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (MGNREGA)** mandates plastic-lined farm ponds under convergence programs.
- **Jal Sanchay Abhiyan** in Kerala uses HDPE-lined check dams and percolation pits.
- **Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY)** subsidizes plastic mulch up to 50 % for small farmers.
- **Israeli drip + mulch** systems achieve 90 %+ water use efficiency — a global benchmark where plastics are central.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Evaporation Reduction Efficiency Under Plastic Mulch

Starting from the basic energy balance at the soil surface:

$$
R_n = G + H + \lambda E
$$

Where:
- $R_n$ = net radiation at soil surface (W·m⁻²)
- $G$ = soil heat flux
- $H$ = sensible heat flux
- $\lambda E$ = latent heat flux (evaporation)

**Step 1: For a bare (un-mulched) soil:**
All incoming radiation reaches the surface, so evaporation is unconstrained:
$$
\lambda E_{\text{open}} = R_n - G - H
$$

**Step 2: For a plastic-mulched soil:**
The film reflects/transmits a fraction $\alpha$ (albedo) and absorbs a fraction $\tau$ of the short-wave radiation. The energy available for soil evaporation becomes:
$$
R_n^{\text{mulch}} = R_n \cdot (1 - \alpha_{\text{film}}) \cdot (1 - \tau_{\text{film}})
$$
And a major share is consumed as sensible heat in the air gap between film and soil:
$$
H_{\text{mulch}} \gg H_{\text{open}}
$$

**Step 3: Net evaporation under mulch:**
$$
\lambda E_{\text{covered}} = R_n^{\text{mulch}} - G_{\text{film}} - H_{\text{mulch}}
$$

**Step 4: Reduction efficiency (substituting the percentage form):**
$$
E_{\text{red}} = \frac{\lambda E_{\text{open}} - \lambda E_{\text{covered}}}{\lambda E_{\text{open}}} \times 100
$$

Since latent heat of vaporization $\lambda$ is constant for both cases, it cancels out:
$$
E_{\text{red}} = \frac{R_n^{\text{open}} - G - H - \left[ R_n^{\text{mulch}} - G_{\text{film}} - H_{\text{mulch}} \right]}{R_n^{\text{open}} - G - H} \times 100
$$

**Step 5: Numerical Example (Typical Values):**
Assume $R_n = 400 \text{ W/m}^2$, $G = 50 \text{ W/m}^2$, $H = 30 \text{ W/m}^2$ for bare soil. For black mulch ($\alpha = 0.10$, $\tau \approx 0$): $R_n^{\text{mulch}} = 36 \text{ W/m}^2$, $G_{\text{film}} = 10$, $H_{\text{mulch}} = 20$.

Substituting:
$$
\lambda E_{\text{open}} = 400 - 50 - 30 = 320 \text{ W/m}^2
$$
$$
\lambda E_{\text{covered}} = 36 - 10 - 20 = 6 \text{ W/m}^2
$$
$$
E_{\text{red}} = \frac{320 - 6}{320} \times 100 = 98.1 \%
$$

In practice, complete cover is not achieved, so field values lie between **40 – 70 %**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Annual Harvested Volume from a Plastic Catchment

Given a catchment area $A$ receiving annual precipitation $P$, with runoff coefficient $C$ and system efficiency $\eta_s$:

**Step 1: Total rainfall volume received**
$$
V_{\text{rain}} = P \cdot A
$$

**Step 2: Volume available as runoff (after infiltration, interception, ET losses)**
$$
V_{\text{runoff}} = C \cdot V_{\text{rain}} = C \cdot P \cdot A
$$

**Step 3: Volume actually harvested at the storage (after conveyance & first-flush losses)**
$$
Q_{\text{harvest}} = \eta_s \cdot V_{\text{runoff}} = \eta_s \cdot C \cdot P \cdot A
$$

**Step 4: Net water available after seepage + evaporation + overflow losses**
$$
S_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{harvest}} - S_{\text{seepage}} - S_{\text{evap}} - O_{\text{overflow}}
$$

**Step 5: Worked Numerical Example**
For a 1000 m² plastic-sheet catchment in Kerala (P = 3000 mm = 3.0 m), with $C = 0.85$ (plastic), $\eta_s = 0.85$:

$$
V_{\text{rain}} = 3.0 \times 1000 = 3000 \text{ m}^3
$$
$$
V_{\text{runoff}} = 0.85 \times 3000 = 2550 \text{ m}^3
$$
$$
Q_{\text{harvest}} = 0.85 \times 2550 = 2167.5 \text{ m}^3 / \text{year}
$$

This is the **gross harvest** before losses; net is lower.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Required Pond Volume for Seasonal Storage

For a pond of trapezoidal cross-section with top width $W_t$, bottom width $W_b$, depth $d$, and side slope $z:1$ (horizontal:vertical):

**Step 1: Top area**
$$
A_1 = W_t \cdot L_t
$$

**Step 2: Bottom area**
$$
A_2 = W_b \cdot L_b = (W_t - 2zd)(L_t - 2zd)
$$

**Step 3: Prismoidal volume (using mean area approximation for shallow depths)**
$$
V = \frac{d}{3} \cdot (A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2})
$$

**Step 4: For rectangular simplification (when $d \ll W$)**
$$
V \approx \frac{d}{2} \cdot (A_1 + A_2)
$$

**Step 5: Numerical**
Take $W_t = 20$ m, $L_t = 20$ m, $d = 3$ m, $z = 1.5$.
- $W_b = 20 - 2(1.5)(3) = 11$ m
- $L_b = 20 - 9 = 11$ m
- $A_1 = 400$ m²
- $A_2 = 121$ m²
- $V = (3/3) \cdot (400 + 121 + \sqrt{400 \times 121}) = 1 \cdot (400 + 121 + 220) = 741$ m³

A **500 µm HDPE liner** of area $(2 \times 20 \times 3) + (2 \times 20 \times 3) + 400 = 760$ m² would seal this pond completely.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting the Right Plastic

| Application | Recommended Polymer | Thickness (µm) | Color | Expected Service Life |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Soil mulching (vegetable) | LLDPE | 25 – 50 | Black / Silver-black | 1 – 3 seasons |
| Soil solarization | LDPE (transparent) | 50 – 100 | Clear | 1 season |
| Orchard floor mulch | HDPE | 100 – 200 | Black | 5 – 8 years |
| Farm pond lining | HDPE | 500 – 1500 | Black (UV-stabilized) | 15 – 25 years |
| Canal lining | PVC / HDPE | 1000 – 2000 | Black | 15 – 20 years |
| Water storage tank | HDPE / FRP | rotational | Opaque white | 10 – 15 years |
| Greenhouse cover | LDPE / EVA | 100 – 200 | Transparent | 3 – 5 years |
| Roof catchment | GI + PVC coating | — | — | 20+ years |

> [!NOTE]
> In **Kerala's high-rainfall coastal zone**, **black LLDPE mulch (25 – 50 µm)** is preferred for vegetables, and **HDPE 750 µm geomembrane** is the BIS standard for farm pond lining (IS 15351:2015 reference, no specific textbook cited).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Plastic-Based In-Situ Moisture Conservation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Solar Radiation + Rainfall Incident on Field] --> B{Plastic Mulch Deployment}
    B -->|Black Film| C[Weed Suppression + Evaporation Block]
    B -->|Transparent Film| D[Soil Solarization + Temp Rise]
    B -->|Silver-Black Film| E[Aphid Repellent + Weed Block]
    C --> F[Moisture Retained in Root Zone]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Increased Water Use Efficiency]
    G --> H[Higher Crop Yield and Quality]
    A --> I[Excess Rainfall]
    I --> J[Raised Bed + Mulch Channels]
    J --> K[In-Situ Storage in Furrows]
    K --> L[Recharge to Sub-Soil Profile]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Plastic-Based Rain Water Harvesting System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Rainfall on Rooftop or Land] --> R2[Plastic or RCC Catchment]
    R2 --> R3[Gutter and Down Pipe - PVC or GI]
    R3 --> R4[First Flush Diverter]
    R4 --> R5[Conveyance to Storage]
    R5 --> R6[HDPE Lined Farm Pond]
    R5 --> R7[HDPE Tank or Ferro Cement Lined Tank]
    R5 --> R8[Recharge Pit with Plastic Filter Media]
    R6 --> R9[Seepage Free Storage]
    R7 --> R9
    R8 --> R10[Groundwater Recharge]
    R9 --> R11[Drip or Sprinkler Irrigation]
    R11 --> R12[Plastic Mulched Field]
    R12 --> R13[Optimum Crop Water Supply]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Functional Block — Water Loss Pathways & Plastic Interventions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    W1[Precipitation Input] --> W2{Loss Pathway}
    W2 --> W21[Evaporation from Soil]
    W2 --> W22[Seepage from Pond]
    W2 --> W23[Runoff Loss from Field]
    W2 --> W24[Deep Percolation Below Root]
    W21 -->|Intervention| I1[Plastic Mulch Film]
    W22 -->|Intervention| I2[HDPE Geomembrane Liner]
    W23 -->|Intervention| I3[Plastic Bunds and Contour Films]
    W24 -->|Intervention| I4[Sub-Surface Plastic Barrier]
    I1 --> O1[Water Saved for Crop ET]
    I2 --> O2[Water Stored in Pond]
    I3 --> O3[Water Infiltrated to Root Zone]
    I4 --> O4[Water Held in Root Zone]
    O1 --> X[Net Improvement in WUE]
    O2 --> X
    O3 --> X
    O4 --> X
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Installation Sequence of HDPE Pond Liner

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Site Survey and Soil Test] --> S2[Excavation to Design Depth]
    S2 --> S3[Subgrade Preparation - Smooth and Compact]
    S3 --> S4[Lay Underlayment - Non-Woven Geotextile]
    S4 --> S5[Unroll HDPE Liner Panels]
    S5 --> S6[Thermal Wedge Welding of Seams]
    S6 --> S7[Air Pressure Test of Seams]
    S7 --> S8[Anchoring Trench Backfill]
    S8 --> S9[Sand or Soil Ballast Layer]
    S9 --> S10[Fill Water Gradually - 0.5 m Per Day]
    S10 --> S11[Operational Pond Ready]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]**
**"List the major plastic films used for in-situ moisture conservation and explain how black plastic mulch conserves soil moisture."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
The major plastic films used for in-situ moisture conservation are:

1. **LDPE (Low-Density Polyethylene)** — flexible, transparent, used for soil solarization.
2. **LLDPE (Linear Low-Density Polyethylene)** — most common for mulching (25–50 µm); high tear resistance.
3. **HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene)** — thicker, used in orchard mulching and pond lining.
4. **EVA (Ethylene Vinyl Acetate)** — used in greenhouse covers and selective mulches.

**Mechanism of moisture conservation by black plastic mulch:**
- The opaque black film **blocks solar radiation** from reaching the soil surface, eliminating the energy source required for evaporation.
- A **saturated micro-climate** is created in the thin air layer between film and soil, reducing the vapor pressure deficit.
- The film also **suppresses weed growth** by blocking photosynthetically active radiation, removing transpirational competition from weeds.
- Field studies report **40 – 70 % reduction in evaporation** and **30 – 50 % savings in irrigation water**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "plastic mulch increases soil temperature" — this is true only for **transparent mulch**. Black mulch *moderates* temperature, sometimes even lowering it by 1 – 2 °C in the surface layer.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]**
**"What is the role of HDPE geomembrane in rain water harvesting? Mention its desirable properties."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
**Role:** HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene) geomembrane is used as an **impermeable liner** in farm ponds, percolation tanks, check dams, and artificial recharge structures. It prevents seepage losses, which in unlined earthen ponds can range from 30 – 60 % of stored water per month in porous soils.

**Desirable Properties of HDPE Geomembrane:**

| Property | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Hydraulic conductivity | **~10⁻¹³ cm/s** | Essentially impermeable |
| Thickness | **500 – 1500 µm** | Puncture resistant |
| UV resistance (with carbon black) | **2 – 2.5 % carbon content** | 20+ year outdoor life |
| Tensile strength | **> 27 MPa** | Withstands subgrade stresses |
| Density | **0.94 – 0.96 g/cc** | Lightweight, easy to install |
| Chemical resistance | Inert to acids, alkalis, salts | Suitable for all soils |
| Weldability | Thermal wedge / hot air | Seamless large panels |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Writing only "HDPE is waterproof" gets **zero** marks. The examiner expects specific quantitative properties and at least 3 reasons why HDPE is preferred over PVC or LDPE for pond lining.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**(a) Explain in detail the various types of plastic mulch used in agriculture, their color significance, and the field installation procedure. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Types of Plastic Mulch (by polymer):**
1. **LDPE mulch** — low-cost, transparent, used mainly in solarization.
2. **LLDPE mulch** — superior mechanical strength, most widely used.
3. **HDPE mulch** — thicker, durable, used in orchards and long-duration crops.
4. **EVA mulch** — selective wavelength transmission, used in specialty crops.
5. **Photodegradable / Biodegradable mulches** — emerging eco-friendly options (starch-blended, PLA-based).

**Color Significance and Spectral Response:**

| Color | Effect on Soil Temp | Effect on Weed | Effect on Pest | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Black | Slight decrease | Excellent block | None | General vegetable mulch |
| Silver-Black | Slight decrease | Excellent | Aphid repellent (UV reflection) | Tomato, capsicum |
| Transparent | Increase 3 – 5 °C | None — must use herbicide | None | Soil solarization |
| White | Neutral / slight decrease | Good | Aphid repellent | Hot summer crops |
| Red / Blue | Marginal change | Good | Selective spectrum | Tomato (red) |
| Reflective (Aluminum) | Decreases | Good | Strong aphid repellent | High-value horticulture |

**Field Installation Procedure:**
1. Land preparation — fine tilth, removal of stones and stubble.
2. Pre-plant irrigation — bring soil to field capacity.
3. Bed formation — raised beds of 1.0 – 1.2 m width with 30 cm furrows.
4. Fumigation (optional) — methyl bromide or solarization.
5. Laying of mulch — manual or machine (plastic mulch laying tractor).
6. Anchoring — bury edges 10 – 15 cm in soil on all sides.
7. Planting holes — punched or burned (black absorbs more heat) at desired spacing.
8. Drip line placement — either sub-mulch (preferred) or surface.
9. Inspection — check for tears, wind uplift.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Tip:** Sub-surface drip + plastic mulch is the highest WUE system, achieving **> 90 % efficiency**.

---

**(b) A farm pond has a rectangular top of 25 m × 20 m, rectangular bottom of 18 m × 13 m, and depth 3.5 m. Calculate (i) the volume of the pond, and (ii) the HDPE liner area required. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Volume using Prismoidal Formula:**

Step 1: Top area
$$
A_1 = 25 \times 20 = 500 \text{ m}^2
$$

Step 2: Bottom area
$$
A_2 = 18 \times 13 = 234 \text{ m}^2
$$

Step 3: Volume
$$
V = \frac{d}{3} \cdot \left(A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 \cdot A_2}\right)
$$
$$
V = \frac{3.5}{3} \cdot \left(500 + 234 + \sqrt{500 \times 234}\right)
$$
$$
V = 1.1667 \cdot (734 + \sqrt{117000})
$$
$$
V = 1.1667 \cdot (734 + 342.05)
$$
$$
V = 1.1667 \times 1076.05
$$
$$
V \approx 1255.4 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Volume ≈ 1255 m³** (or ~12.55 lakh litres)

**(ii) HDPE Liner Area:**

Step 1: Slant length of longer side (sides slope uniformly). Assume 1:1 side slope:
- Run = (25 − 18)/2 = 3.5 m horizontal
- Rise = 3.5 m vertical
- Slant length = $\sqrt{3.5^2 + 3.5^2} = 3.5\sqrt{2} \approx 4.95$ m

Step 2: Slant length of shorter side:
- Run = (20 − 13)/2 = 3.5 m
- Slant length = $3.5\sqrt{2} \approx 4.95$ m (same)

Step 3: Slant areas
- Two long sides: $2 \times 25 \times 4.95 = 247.5$ m²
- Two short sides: $2 \times 20 \times 4.95 = 198.0$ m²

Step 4: Top + Bottom (with 0.5 m anchor overlap on each side, adding 1 m on each dimension):
- Top with anchor: $(25 + 1) \times (20 + 1) = 26 \times 21 = 546$ m²
- Bottom: $18 \times 13 = 234$ m²

Step 5: Total liner area
$$
A_{\text{liner}} = 247.5 + 198.0 + 546 + 234 = 1225.5 \text{ m}^2
$$

Add 10 % wastage for seam overlaps and wastage:
$$
A_{\text{required}} = 1225.5 \times 1.10 \approx 1348 \text{ m}^2
$$

**HDPE Liner (750 µm thickness) Required ≈ 1348 m²** (i.e., 14 rolls of standard 100 m² size, or as available in market rolls).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors:** (1) Forgetting to add anchor trench allowance. (2) Using only top area as liner (this would leave bottom exposed). (3) Confusing 750 µm with 750 mm — it's **0.75 mm**, not 0.75 cm.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**(a) Describe the components of a roof-top rain water harvesting system using plastics, with a neat flow description. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A typical plastic-based roof-top rain water harvesting system consists of the following components:

**1. Catchment Surface (Roof)**
- Corrugated GI, AC, or RCC roof treated with **food-grade plastic coating or epoxy paint** to prevent leaching.
- Plastic tiles/sheets are increasingly used in polyhouse-integrated systems.

**2. Gutters and Down-Comers**
- **uPVC or PVC gutters** of 100 – 150 mm diameter.
- **HDPE/PVC down-pipes** (75 – 110 mm) fixed along the wall.

**3. First-Flush Diverter**
- A **plastic chamber (HDPE)** that discards the first 2 – 5 mm of rain to remove dust, bird droppings, and pollutant load.

**4. Conveyance System**
- **MDPE/HDPE pipes** (ISI-marked, PN 6 or PN 10 class) carry filtered water to storage.

**5. Filter Unit**
- **Plastic-housed sand filter** and **activated carbon filter** for turbidity and odor removal.
- Often combined with a **plastic mesh screen (1 mm aperture)** to trap debris.

**6. Storage Tank**
- **HDPE tanks** (capacity 1000 – 10000 L) — most common in Kerala households.
- **Ferro-cement tanks with HDPE liner** — larger community scale.
- **Underground sump** with HDPE lining for institutional scale.

**7. Recharge Structure (optional)**
- **Recharge pit** filled with gravel, sand, and wrapped in **non-woven geotextile plastic fabric** to allow percolation but exclude fines.

**Flow Description:**
Rainfall → Roof → Gutter → First-Flush Diverter → Filter → Storage Tank → (Pump) → Usage / Recharge Pit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerala Context:** Under the **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** and **Mukhyamantri Vasudha Scheme**, plastic-based RWH structures are promoted in schools and public buildings for groundwater augmentation.

---

**(b) A village in Palakkad has an annual rainfall of 2200 mm. A plastic-lined farm pond of dimensions 30 m × 25 m × 4 m (rectangular) is proposed. The runoff coefficient of the catchment (laterite soil) is 0.30, and system efficiency is 0.80. Estimate (i) annual runoff harvested, (ii) the number of fillings of the pond per year, and (iii) the seepage loss in one month if the pond were unlined. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- $P = 2200$ mm $= 2.2$ m
- Catchment area — not given; assume a contributing catchment of 1 hectare (10,000 m²) for an unstated but standard estimate. (If the catchment area is assumed equal to the pond top area, calculations differ — see note below.)
- $C = 0.30$
- $\eta_s = 0.80$
- Pond volume: $30 \times 25 \times 4 = 3000$ m³

**Case 1: Catchment A = 10,000 m² (1 ha)**

**(i) Annual runoff harvested:**
$$
V_{\text{rain}} = P \cdot A = 2.2 \times 10000 = 22000 \text{ m}^3
$$
$$
V_{\text{runoff}} = C \cdot V_{\text{rain}} = 0.30 \times 22000 = 6600 \text{ m}^3
$$
$$
Q_{\text{harvest}} = \eta_s \cdot V_{\text{runoff}} = 0.80 \times 6600 = 5280 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}
$$

**(ii) Number of fillings:**
$$
N = \frac{Q_{\text{harvest}}}{V_{\text{pond}}} = \frac{5280}{3000} = 1.76
$$

**The pond fills approximately 1.76 times per year.**

**(iii) Seepage loss per month (unlined):**
Use Darcy's law for unlined laterite pond. Assume:
- $K_{\text{laterite}} = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s (typical)
- Wetted area (avg.) = top + bottom + sides / 2
- Pond surface area average = $\frac{30 \times 25 + 30 \times 25}{2} = 750$ m² (assume negligible depth on area change)
- Sides: $2 \times (30 \times 4) + 2 \times (25 \times 4) = 240 + 200 = 440$ m²
- Bottom: $30 \times 25 = 750$ m²
- Total wetted perimeter area $= 750 + 440 + 750 = 1940$ m²

Hydraulic gradient $\frac{dh}{dx} \approx 1$ (vertical percolation, unit gradient for shallow ponds).

$$
S_{\text{seepage}} = K \cdot A \cdot \frac{dh}{dx} \cdot t
$$
$$
= 1 \times 10^{-5} \times 1940 \times 1 \times (30 \times 24 \times 3600)
$$
$$
= 1 \times 10^{-5} \times 1940 \times 2592000
$$
$$
= 1 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.028 \times 10^{9}
$$
$$
= 50280 \text{ m}^3 / \text{month}
$$

This is **physically impossible** (more than 17 times the pond volume!) — confirming that **plastic lining is essential** to retain water.

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Trap:** Many students forget the time unit. $K$ is in m/s, so $t$ must be in **seconds**, not days. (30 days = 30 × 86400 = 2,592,000 s). Also, if a smaller catchment area is given, the answer scales down accordingly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Plastic in Water Management (Module 4)**

- **Mulch films** are typically **25 – 50 µm LLDPE**, color-dependent function.
- **Black mulch** → weed control; **Transparent** → solarization; **Silver-black** → pest repelling.
- **Evaporation reduction** by mulch = **40 – 70 %** in field conditions.
- **HDPE geomembrane** is the BIS standard (IS 15351) for farm pond lining, thickness **500 – 1500 µm**.
- **Hydraulic conductivity of HDPE** ≈ **10⁻¹³ cm/s** (essentially zero seepage).
- **Runoff coefficient (C):** Plastic roof = 0.80 – 0.85, soil = 0.20 – 0.40, concrete = 0.70 – 0.90.
- **Key formula:** $Q_{\text{harvest}} = P \cdot A \cdot C \cdot \eta_s$
- **Pond volume (trapezoidal):** $V = \dfrac{d}{3}(A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2})$
- **Prismoidal vs Mean Area approximation** — mean area used when $d$ is small.
- **First-flush diverter** is essential to discard initial polluted runoff.
- **Non-woven geotextile** under liner prevents puncture from subgrade.
- **Carbon black (2 – 2.5 %)** in HDPE ensures UV resistance and 20+ year life.
- **Kerala-specific applications:** Coconut basin mulch, pepper vine mulching, cardamom bed mulch, roof-top RWH in schools.
- **Government schemes:** PMKSY (mulch subsidy), MGNREGA (pond excavation), Jal Jeevan Mission (RWH).
- **Environmental concern:** Disposal of used mulch film; **biodegradable and photodegradable mulches** are the emerging solution.
- **Biodegradable mulches** — starch-blended, PLA, paper-based; decompose in 60 – 120 days.
- **Service life ordering:** LDPE < LLDPE < HDPE < PVC < PP < FRP (for water-contact applications).
- **Drip + mulch** = highest WUE combo in field (> 90 %).
- **Always add 10 – 15 %** to liner area calculation for wastage and anchor trench.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastic Film Lining in Canal, Pond and Reservoir
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT611 - PLASTIC APPLICATION IN AGRICULTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plastic Film Lining in Canal, Pond and Reservoir

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Plastic Film Lining** is the technique of installing a thin, continuous, impermeable synthetic polymeric membrane on the prepared subgrade of hydraulic conveyance and storage structures (canals, ponds, reservoirs, and tanks) to **prevent or drastically reduce seepage losses**, **control sub-surface drainage**, and **enhance water-use efficiency** in irrigated agriculture.

According to KTU 2024 (NEP 2020) Outcome-Based Education standards, plastic film lining is classified as a *Geosynthetic Hydraulic Barrier Application* under the broader umbrella of *Plasticulture in Water Resource Engineering*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4 (Plasticulture in Water Management):**
> Plastic film lining is the **primary engineered intervention** for reducing conveyance losses in unlined earthen channels (which typically lose **30% to 50%** of total diverted water to seepage in sandy/alluvial soils) and for securing harvested rainwater in farm reservoirs.

### 1.2 Standard Plastic Films Used in Lining (KTU Reference Materials)

| Polymer | Full Form | Typical Thickness | KTU-Standard Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HDPE** | High-Density Polyethylene | 0.5 mm – 2.5 mm | Reservoir & large canal liners |
| **LLDPE** | Linear Low-Density Polyethylene | 0.2 mm – 1.0 mm | Pond lining (flexible) |
| **LDPE** | Low-Density Polyethylene | 0.1 mm – 0.5 mm | Low-cost farm pond lining |
| **PVC** | Polyvinyl Chloride | 0.5 mm – 1.5 mm | Specialized canal lining |
| **EPDM / Butyl Rubber** | Ethylene Propylene Diene Monomer | 1.0 mm – 2.0 mm | Long-life reservoir lining |

> [!NOTE]
> **Permissible Permeability Coefficient (k) Range for Lining Films:**
> A *good* plastic lining must exhibit a hydraulic conductivity **k < 1 × 10⁻¹² cm/s**, ensuring it acts as a near-perfect hydraulic barrier (effectively zero seepage).

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Bathtub Liner Principle"

Imagine your bathroom bathtub. The tub itself is made of porous concrete (analogous to an unlined earthen canal). Water seeps through slowly, and over time, the floor below becomes damp and structurally weak. The solution in your bathroom? **A thin, continuous vinyl/PVC sheet (the liner) glued to the inside of the tub.** Water now never touches the porous concrete — it only touches the impermeable film.

**Plastic film lining of canals/ponds/reservoirs works on EXACTLY the same principle:**

- The **earthen subgrade** = the porous concrete tub
- The **plastic film** = the bathtub liner
- The **protective soil cover** = the tile/grout on top of the liner
- The **irrigation water** = the bathwater that stays inside

Just as a single pinhole in a bathtub liner causes a leak, a **single puncture** in a canal/pond film can compromise the entire lining system. This is why installation care, seam welding, and puncture protection are critical quality parameters in KTU practical examinations.

### 1.4 Why Plastic Film Lining? (Engineering Necessity)

- **Seepage Control**: Unlined canals in sandy soils can lose **15% to 50%** of the total water diverted from the source.
- **Water Table Control**: Prevents waterlogging and secondary salinization in command areas.
- **Structural Stability**: Reduces internal erosion and piping failures in embankments.
- **Evaporation Reduction** (when combined with floating covers): Up to **80% to 90%** reduction in evaporative loss.
- **Economic Efficiency**: Cheaper and faster to install than concrete or clay liners.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics to Remember (Bolded):**
> - Density of HDPE ≈ **0.94 – 0.97 g/cm³**
> - Density of LDPE/LLDPE ≈ **0.91 – 0.93 g/cm³**
> - Solar reflectance of black HDPE film ≈ **5% – 10%** (excellent UV protection for buried films)
> - **Permissible in-situ permeability of HDPE liner ≤ 1 × 10⁻¹² cm/s**
> - Service life of HDPE liner (buried) ≈ **20 – 30 years** (KTU standard)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Concept — Layered Logic Breakdown

A plastic film lining system is **not** just a sheet of plastic thrown into a canal. It is a **multi-layered engineered system** with each layer performing a distinct, non-negotiable function. KTU expects students to understand the role of every layer.

**Layered Architecture (Top → Bottom, as installed in field):**

1. **Protective Soil Cover (Top Layer)** — *Why?* Prevents UV degradation, mechanical puncture by animals/foot traffic, and wind uplift. Typical depth: **200 mm – 300 mm**.
2. **Geotextile Cushion / Sand Cushion (Optional Middle Layer)** — *Why?* Distributes point loads and prevents sharp subgrade particles from puncturing the film.
3. **Plastic Film Liner (Primary Barrier)** — *Why?* The actual impermeable layer that physically blocks water movement.
4. **Prepared Subgrade (Bottom Layer)** — *Why?* A smooth, compacted, debris-free surface (compaction ≥ 95% Standard Proctor) that ensures uniform film seating.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept Check:** Without a smooth subgrade, the film experiences **stress concentrations** at sharp aggregate points. These lead to *puncture failures* — the most common reason plastic-lined canals fail in field service.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet for Board Exams)

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\large Q_s = K \cdot i \cdot A$ | Darcy's seepage law: $Q_s$ = seepage discharge, $K$ = hydraulic conductivity, $i$ = hydraulic gradient, $A$ = cross-section area | Estimating seepage loss in unlined canal |
| 2 | $\large K_s = \dfrac{Q_s}{i \cdot A}$ | Re-arranged Darcy to compute conductivity | Comparing soil vs. film permeability |
| 3 | $\large V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | Manning's equation: $V$ = velocity, $n$ = Manning's roughness, $R$ = hydraulic radius, $S$ = bed slope | Flow capacity of lined canal |
| 4 | $\large \Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion: $\alpha$ = linear thermal expansion coefficient, $L$ = original length, $\Delta T$ = temperature change | Computing expansion slack in summer installation |
| 5 | $\large t_{min} = \dfrac{P \cdot d}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}}$ | Required film thickness $t_{min}$: $P$ = hydrostatic pressure, $d$ = diameter of subgrade protrusion, $\sigma_{allow}$ = allowable tensile stress of film | Puncture-resistance design check |
| 6 | $\large E = \dfrac{\gamma \cdot d^4}{8 \cdot D^2}$ | Maximum deflection of film under self-weight and water load: $\gamma$ = unit weight, $d$ = span, $D$ = flexural rigidity | Settlement check for reservoir liner |
| 7 | $\large S_{eff} = \dfrac{\text{Water delivered}}{\text{Water diverted}}$ | Conveyance efficiency of lined canal | Comparing lined vs. unlined performance |
| 8 | $\large BCR = \dfrac{\sum P_V(\text{Benefits})}{\sum P_V(\text{Costs})}$ | Benefit–Cost Ratio (BCR): $P_V$ = present value | Economic justification of lining project |
| 9 | $\large t = t_{design} \cdot f_s$ | Design thickness with safety factor $f_s$ (typically **1.5 – 2.0**) | Final specification of liner thickness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coefficient of Linear Thermal Expansion ($\alpha$) — KTU Standard Values:**
> - HDPE: $\alpha \approx \mathbf{1.2 \times 10^{-4} \, /^{\circ}C}$
> - PVC: $\alpha \approx \mathbf{7.0 \times 10^{-5} \, /^{\circ}C}$
> - LLDPE: $\alpha \approx \mathbf{1.5 \times 10^{-4} \, /^{\circ}C}$
>
> **Consequence:** A 10 m long HDPE film installed at 20°C in winter and exposed to 50°C in summer will expand by **ΔL = 1.2 × 10⁻⁴ × 10 × 30 ≈ 0.036 m (36 mm)**. Hence, **expansion slack must be provided** during installation.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Maharashtra & Gujarat Lift-Irrigation Schemes (India):** Large HDPE-lined canals of the *Bhima* and *Narmada* projects transport water over hundreds of kilometers, saving millions of cubic meters annually.
- **Pond Lining in Arid Rajasthan:** LDPE film (0.1 mm) lined farm ponds form the backbone of *Jal Swavalamban* (rainwater harvesting) programs.
- **Reservoir Linings Worldwide:** The 2.0 mm HDPE liner is the global standard for potable water reservoirs and effluent treatment lagoons.

### 2.4 Comparative Performance: Lined vs. Unlined Canal

| Performance Parameter | Unlined Earthen Canal | Plastic Film Lined Canal |
|---|---|---|
| Seepage loss (% of flow) | 30% – 50% | < 1% |
| Manning's roughness $n$ | 0.025 – 0.035 | 0.012 – 0.015 (much smoother) |
| Conveyance efficiency | 50% – 70% | 90% – 95% |
| Maintenance cost | High (silting, weed growth) | Low |
| Service life | 5 – 10 years | 20 – 30 years |
| Initial cost | Low | Moderate to high |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are **fully expanded step-by-step** with no skipped algebra. This section is the KTU 14-mark backbone for Part B questions on seepage estimation and lining thickness design.

### 3.1 Derivation I — Seepage Loss Reduction by Lining

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-mark format):**
A trapezoidal earthen canal passes through sandy loam soil. The canal dimensions are: bottom width $b = 1.5$ m, side slope $z = 1.5 : 1$, depth of flow $y = 1.2$ m, longitudinal slope $S = 0.0008$, and length $L = 4$ km. The hydraulic conductivity of the subsoil is $K = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ cm/s. Compute (a) the seepage loss in the unlined canal per day, and (b) the seepage loss after lining with HDPE film (assume film permeability $K_f = 1 \times 10^{-12}$ cm/s).

#### Step 1 — Compute the Wetted Cross-Sectional Area of the Canal

The cross-section is a trapezoid:
$A = b \cdot y + z \cdot y^{2}$

Substitute $b = 1.5$ m, $y = 1.2$ m, $z = 1.5$:
$A = (1.5 \times 1.2) + (1.5 \times 1.2^{2})$
$A = 1.80 + 1.5 \times 1.44$
$A = 1.80 + 2.16 = 3.96 \, \text{m}^{2}$

#### Step 2 — Compute the Wetted Perimeter

$P = b + 2y\sqrt{1 + z^{2}}$
$P = 1.5 + 2(1.2)\sqrt{1 + 1.5^{2}}$
$P = 1.5 + 2.4 \times \sqrt{3.25}$
$P = 1.5 + 2.4 \times 1.8028 = 1.5 + 4.3267 = 5.8267 \, \text{m}$

#### Step 3 — Compute the Hydraulic Radius

$R = \dfrac{A}{P} = \dfrac{3.96}{5.8267} = 0.6796 \, \text{m}$

#### Step 4 — Compute the Hydraulic Gradient in the Subsoil

For an unlined canal, the hydraulic gradient $i$ in the subsoil (assuming no downstream saturation barrier) is approximately equal to the **longitudinal slope** for steady-state flow:
$i \approx S = 0.0008$

#### Step 5 — Apply Darcy's Law to Compute Seepage Discharge (Unlined)

Darcy's equation: $Q_s = K \cdot i \cdot A_{sub}$

Convert $K$ to SI units: $K = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm/s} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s}$

For a per-unit-length calculation, $A_{sub} \approx w \cdot 1$ (perimeter wetted × 1 m length):
Effective seepage perimeter $\approx P = 5.8267$ m (acts over 1 m length → area = 5.8267 m²)

$Q_s = (5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.0008 \times 5.8267$
$Q_s = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 4.6614 \times 10^{-3}$
$Q_s = 2.3307 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s per meter length}$

For the full canal length of 4 km = 4000 m:
$Q_{s,\text{total}} = 2.3307 \times 10^{-8} \times 4000 = 9.3228 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$

Convert to cubic meters per day:
$Q_{s,\text{day}} = 9.3228 \times 10^{-5} \times 86400 = 8.055 \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{day}$

#### Step 6 — Recompute Seepage Loss After HDPE Lining

With HDPE film, the effective permeability is now $K_f = 1 \times 10^{-12}$ cm/s = $1 \times 10^{-14}$ m/s:

$Q_{s,\text{lined}} = (1 \times 10^{-14}) \times 0.0008 \times 5.8267 \times 4000$
$Q_{s,\text{lined}} = 1.864 \times 10^{-13} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$
$Q_{s,\text{day,lined}} = 1.864 \times 10^{-13} \times 86400 = 1.611 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{day}$

#### Step 7 — Compute the Percentage Reduction in Seepage

$\text{Reduction \%} = \dfrac{Q_{s,\text{unlined}} - Q_{s,\text{lined}}}{Q_{s,\text{unlined}}} \times 100$
$\text{Reduction \%} = \dfrac{8.055 - 1.611 \times 10^{-8}}{8.055} \times 100 \approx 99.9999998\%$

> **Interpretation:** HDPE lining reduces seepage to **essentially zero** — a < 0.0000002% loss. This is the engineering basis of the KTU standard specification.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Required Film Thickness (Puncture Resistance)

**Given:** Hydrostatic pressure head $h = 1.5$ m, subgrade protrusion diameter $d = 10$ mm, allowable tensile stress of HDPE film $\sigma_{allow} = 17$ N/mm². Compute the minimum film thickness $t_{min}$.

#### Step 1 — Convert Pressure into Force per Unit Area

Hydrostatic pressure: $P = \gamma_w \cdot h = 9810 \times 1.5 = 14715 \, \text{N/m}^{2} = 0.014715 \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$

#### Step 2 — Apply the Puncture Resistance Formula

$t_{min} = \dfrac{P \cdot d}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}}$
$t_{min} = \dfrac{0.014715 \times 10}{2 \times 17}$
$t_{min} = \dfrac{0.14715}{34} = 0.004328 \, \text{mm}$

#### Step 3 — Apply the Safety Factor

For field installation, KTU standards demand a **safety factor $f_s = 2.0$** (to account for UV, puncture, welding, and long-term creep):

$t_{design} = t_{min} \times f_s = 0.004328 \times 2.0 = 0.00866 \, \text{mm}$

#### Step 4 — Round Up to the Next KTU-Standard Gauge

KTU standard HDPE gauges: 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5 mm.
$t_{selected} = 0.5 \, \text{mm}$ (the next standard gauge above 0.00866 mm).

> **Conclusion:** A **0.5 mm HDPE film** is the engineering minimum for this case. This is also the KTU-recommended minimum for canal lining in field conditions.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference for Exam Day

| Quantity | Unlined Canal | HDPE Lined Canal | % Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Seepage loss (m³/day) | 8.055 | ~ 0 | ~ 100% |
| Manning's $n$ | 0.030 (assumed) | 0.014 | 53% lower |
| Flow velocity (m/s) | 0.86 | 1.32 | 53% higher |
| Cross-section area needed (for same Q) | 3.96 m² | ~ 2.58 m² | 35% saving |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define plastic film lining for canals. List any three commonly used plastic films with their typical thickness range.**

**Model Answer:**
Plastic film lining is the technique of covering the prepared subgrade of a canal, pond, or reservoir with a thin, impermeable synthetic polymer membrane to prevent seepage losses and improve water conveyance efficiency.

Three commonly used plastic films:
- **HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene):** 0.5 mm – 2.5 mm — used for large canals and reservoirs.
- **LLDPE (Linear Low-Density Polyethylene):** 0.2 mm – 1.0 mm — used for farm ponds.
- **LDPE (Low-Density Polyethylene):** 0.1 mm – 0.5 mm — used for low-cost farm pond lining.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **State any three advantages of plastic film lining over conventional concrete lining in canals.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Lower initial cost:** Plastic film lining is typically 30%–50% cheaper per square meter than concrete lining, especially for large canals.
2. **Higher seepage control:** HDPE film has permeability $k \leq 1 \times 10^{-12}$ cm/s, which is far lower than even well-built concrete.
3. **Faster installation:** Plastic film rolls can be unrolled and welded in days, while concrete lining requires weeks of formwork, curing, and joint sealing.
4. **Flexibility:** Plastic films can conform to subgrade settlements and minor deformations without cracking (unlike rigid concrete, which cracks on settlement).

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — Canal Lining Design Problem

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Model Question Bank]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] An unlined trapezoidal canal in sandy loam has the following data: bottom width $b = 2.0$ m, side slope $z = 1:1$, depth of flow $y = 1.5$ m, bed slope $S = 0.001$, length $L = 5$ km, and subsoil hydraulic conductivity $K = 8 \times 10^{-4}$ cm/s. Calculate the **daily seepage loss** of the canal.

**(b)** [7 Marks] If the same canal is lined with 1.0 mm HDPE film (permeability $K_f = 1 \times 10^{-13}$ cm/s), calculate the **seepage loss after lining**. Comment on the percentage reduction in seepage and its impact on the **conveyance efficiency** of the canal.

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional Area:**
$A = by + zy^2 = (2.0 \times 1.5) + (1 \times 1.5^2) = 3.0 + 2.25 = 5.25 \, \text{m}^2$

**Step 2 — Wetted Perimeter:**
$P = b + 2y\sqrt{1 + z^2} = 2.0 + 2(1.5)\sqrt{1 + 1} = 2.0 + 3.0 \times 1.4142 = 2.0 + 4.2426 = 6.2426 \, \text{m}$

**Step 3 — Hydraulic Gradient:**
$i = S = 0.001$

**Step 4 — Convert K to SI units:**
$K = 8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm/s} = 8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s}$

**Step 5 — Apply Darcy's Law per meter length (using wetted perimeter as effective seepage area):**
$q_s = K \cdot i \cdot P = 8 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.001 \times 6.2426 = 4.994 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s per m}$

**Step 6 — Total seepage for 5 km length:**
$Q_s = 4.994 \times 10^{-8} \times 5000 = 2.497 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$

**Step 7 — Daily seepage loss:**
$Q_{s,\text{day}} = 2.497 \times 10^{-4} \times 86400 = 21.57 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Convert film permeability to SI:**
$K_f = 1 \times 10^{-13} \text{ cm/s} = 1 \times 10^{-15} \text{ m/s}$

**Step 2 — Apply Darcy's Law with new K:**
$q_{s,\text{lined}} = 1 \times 10^{-15} \times 0.001 \times 6.2426 = 6.2426 \times 10^{-18} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s per m}$

**Step 3 — Total daily seepage after lining:**
$Q_{s,\text{day,lined}} = 6.2426 \times 10^{-18} \times 5000 \times 86400 = 2.697 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$

**Step 4 — Percentage Reduction:**
$\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{21.57 - 2.697 \times 10^{-9}}{21.57} \times 100 \approx 99.9999999875\%$

**Step 5 — Impact on Conveyance Efficiency:**
Conveyance efficiency increases from approximately **60%–70%** (unlined) to **92%–95%** (HDPE lined). For a command area of 100 ha, this saving translates to an additional **~25% water available at the farm gate** during the rabi season, directly increasing crop yield.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Pond and Reservoir Lining Theory

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with a neat block diagram the **layered cross-section** of a plastic film lined farm pond. State the function of each layer.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare **HDPE, LLDPE, and LDPE** as liner materials with respect to (i) density, (ii) tensile strength, (iii) UV resistance, (iv) flexibility, and (v) cost. State the appropriate KTU-recommended application for each.

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Layered Cross-Section of a Plastic Film Lined Farm Pond** (from inside, water-side → outside):

| Layer | Material | Thickness | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (innermost) | Water | — | Water storage |
| 2 | Protective soil cover (sand + clay) | 200–300 mm | UV protection, mechanical protection |
| 3 | HDPE / LLDPE film | 0.5–1.5 mm | Primary impermeable barrier |
| 4 | Sand cushion (fine, washed) | 50–100 mm | Puncture protection from subgrade |
| 5 | Geotextile (optional) | 2–3 mm | Separation & additional cushioning |
| 6 (outermost) | Compacted subgrade (95% Proctor) | — | Structural foundation |

**Function of each layer explained:**

1. **Protective Soil Cover:** Shields the film from UV radiation (which degrades polymers), prevents wind uplift, and protects from animal/foot puncture. Standard depth: **200–300 mm**.
2. **Plastic Film Liner:** The actual hydraulic barrier with $k \leq 10^{-12}$ cm/s; blocks all seepage.
3. **Sand Cushion:** Distributes point loads from subgrade and prevents sharp stones from puncturing the liner.
4. **Compacted Subgrade:** Provides a smooth, stable platform for the liner; compaction ≥ 95% Standard Proctor is KTU-mandated.

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

| Property | HDPE | LLDPE | LDPE |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Density | 0.94–0.97 g/cm³ (highest) | 0.91–0.93 g/cm³ (medium) | 0.91–0.93 g/cm³ (lowest) |
| (ii) Tensile strength | Very high (25–35 MPa) | High (20–28 MPa) | Lower (10–18 MPa) |
| (iii) UV resistance | Excellent (with 2%–3% carbon black) | Very good | Moderate |
| (iv) Flexibility | Lower (stiffer) | Very high (most flexible) | High (flexible) |
| (v) Cost per m² (India, 2024) | ₹120–180 | ₹90–140 | ₹50–90 |
| **KTU Application** | Large reservoirs & main canals | Medium farm ponds | Small farm ponds, low-budget projects |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up units:** Converting hydraulic conductivity $K$ from cm/s to m/s is the single biggest numerical error. **Always remember: divide cm/s by 100 to get m/s.**
> 2. **Forgetting safety factor:** Students often compute $t_{min}$ but forget to apply the safety factor $f_s = 2.0$. The examiner deducts 1 mark for this.
> 3. **Confusing Darcy's law variables:** Students sometimes write $Q = K \cdot A$ (forgetting $i$). Always include the hydraulic gradient.
> 4. **Skipping unit labels in final answers:** KTU deducts 0.5 marks per numerical answer if units (m³/day, mm, MPa) are missing.
> 5. **Not labeling layers in diagrams:** A neat block diagram without thickness labels loses 1 mark in Part (a) drawing questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Quick-Reference for the Last-Night Revision:**

- **Definition:** Plastic film lining = thin impermeable polymer membrane on canal/pond/reservoir subgrade to block seepage.
- **Most common films:** HDPE (≥ 0.5 mm), LLDPE (0.2–1.0 mm), LDPE (0.1–0.5 mm), PVC (0.5–1.5 mm).
- **Permeability standard:** $k \leq 1 \times 10^{-12}$ cm/s for a *good* liner.
- **Unlined seepage loss:** **30%–50%** of total flow; Lined seepage loss: **< 1%**.
- **Manning's $n$:** Unlined = 0.025–0.035; Lined = 0.012–0.015.
- **Thermal expansion of HDPE:** $\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \, /^{\circ}C$ — always provide expansion slack.
- **Standard layer order (top→bottom):** Protective soil cover → Liner → Sand cushion → Subgrade.
- **Subgrade compaction requirement:** ≥ **95% Standard Proctor density**.
- **Anchor trench depth:** 0.5 m × 0.5 m at the top of bank.
- **Seam overlap:** **100–150 mm**; Seam test: Air pressure 0.2 MPa for **5 minutes**.
- **Design safety factor for film thickness:** **$f_s = 1.5$ to 2.0**.
- **Service life of HDPE liner (buried):** **20–30 years**.
- **Conveyance efficiency gain:** From ~60% (unlined) to ~92% (HDPE lined) — a **+30%** water saving.
- **Most common KTU exam problem:** "Compute seepage loss before and after lining" — use Darcy's law.
- **Most common definition question:** "Define plastic film lining" — 2 lines; 1 mark.
- **Most common comparison question:** HDPE vs. LLDPE vs. LDPE — know the application matrix by heart.
- **Practical tip:** Always write units and never skip the safety factor.
- **Black color HDPE** is preferred for buried applications (carbon black provides UV protection).
- **White/transparent films** are used for *floating covers* on reservoirs (to control algae & evaporation).
- **KTU Module 4 emphasis:** Lining is a water-management application of plasticulture — focus on water saving, not aesthetics.
